condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Relevant CPD 2021-2025 Output Indicator: 4.3.3. Number of technology solutions used for management of harmful chemicals

description.narrative:
Indicators 1st Relevant CPD 2021-2025 Output Indicator: 4.3.3. Number of technology solutions used for management of harmful chemicals Project Output Indicator 1: Adoption/implementation of ODS legislation and regulation to control and monitor ODS consumption Project Output Indicator 2: Efficient and timely data collection and reporting Project Output Indicator 3: Consultations and coordination with other national gencies/stakeholders Project Output Indicator 4: Supervision of timely implementation of phase-out activities and reduction in ODS consumption Project Output Indicator 5: Awareness and Publication Project Output Indicator 6: Regional cooperation and participation in Montreal Protocol meetings
Data validates that Indicative CP output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the CPD (2020), through project intervention. The project supported the Directorate of Climate Change Mitigation in the development of the REKOMBPO application and MontiR-AC application. Those applications are used for the mitigation of ODS for importers and HFCs in the domestic sector. Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. 1.a Facilitating technical meetings related to data mechanism delivery between SNANK (Sistem Nasional Neraca Komoditas) and the REKOMBPO application on February 9, 2023, and May 31, 2023. 1.b Facilitating a meeting related to the formulation of an Urgency/Explanation Paper for Implementation Regulations as the Implementation of the Montreal Protocol on February 17, 2023.
1.c Supporting Indonesian Delegate for Kigali Implementation Plan (KIP) Preparation Workshop on February 27, 2023. 1.d Facilitating a meeting related to the Implementation of Montreal Protocol Regulations on March 6, 2023. 1.e Facilitating a series of meetings related to formulation Ministerial Regulation concerning Procedures of ODS and HFC Recommendations on April 5, 2023; April 13, 2023; May 19, 2023; May 24-25, 2023; June 14, 2023, and June 20, 2023. 1.f Facilitating a meeting related to changes in Guidelines and Technical Guidelines of Operational Assistance to the System House Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. 2.a Facilitating a meeting for the preparation of ODS and HFC Consumption Reports to the Multilateral Fund and Ozone Secretariate on April 17-18, 2023. 2.b Convening a HFC Mapping Kick-Off Meeting on May 23, 2023. 2.c Facilitating a meeting related to the HFC Report for the year 2020-2021 on June 9, 2023.
Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. Facilitating a meeting related to formulation Explanation Paper for the preparation of the draft of Presidential Regulation on National Policy and Strategy for ODS and HFC regulation on February 10, 2023. Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. The project will conduct supervision related to ODS and HFC consumption in the second semester of 2023. Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. Convening a socialization meeting on the Presidential Regulation Number 129 of 2022 concerning The Ratification of the Amendment to the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, Kigali, 2016 on January 25, 2023. This activity successfully covered by 19 media coverage accros Indonesia, and 1 (one) press release published by Bureau of Public Relations Ministry of Environment and Forestry. Data validates that output indicators is partially achieved and expected to be fully achieved by the end of the year. Supporting Indonesian Delegate for the forty-fifth meeting of the Open-ended Working Group (OEWG45) of the Parties to the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
INDICATOR #1 The number of innovative financial instruments piloted and tested: blended waqf-linked sukuk which invests cash waqf in a socially responsible sukuk; digital platform for waqf collection and distribution to SDG related initiatives; SDG Impact Fund Negara focused on investing in women-led social enterprises in the agribusiness value chain. (which include Islamic Finance models)
INDICATOR #2 The policies developed to support innovative financing, the training, advice and support provided, and by the number of partners engaged. (e.g. Waqf Core principles approved and implemented; Zakat for SDGs guidance approved and implemented; OJK Roadmap for Sustainable Finance informed by SDGs; a more conducive licensing for Social Finance.

comment.narrative:
INDICATOR #1 BASELINE: Zero innovative financial instrument of waqf-linked sukuk; Zero SDG Impact Fund which focused on investing in women-led social enterprises in the agribusiness value chain; Zero block-chain digitally enhanced platform to mobilize Social and Islamic funding to achieve SDGs.
INDICATOR #2 BASELINE: Zero improved policies, regulations and programs to support innovative financing for SDGs. 20 partners from government, private sectors and religious organizations.
- Climate Change Fiscal Framework (CCFF) development started with government partners - One study on impact of COVID-19 to climate finance - One impact report for green investment projects

- The kick off meeting of CCFF development convened in October 2020 led by MoF. - Supported the MoF in developing a study on the impact of COVID-19 on GHG emissions from the energy sector as well as the analysis of the public climate financing post-COVID-19. - The second Green Sukuk Allocation and Impact Report was published in March 2020, with support from UNDP.
- Final Draft of CCFF - One study on innovative finance - One impact report for green investment projects

- In collaboration with other development partners (GGGI and GIZ), the final draft of CCFF has been developed and to be presented to Chairman of BKF - MOF. - The project, in collaboration with the Islamic Finance Council UK (UKIFC) developed a study titled “Innovation in Islamic Finance: Green Sukuk for SDGs”, providing a practical orientation on how Islamic Finance can be leveraged for countries’ climate and sustainable development agenda. - The project supported towards the development of Indonesia’s 3rd Green Sukuk Allocation and Impact Report, published in May 2021
- Public launching of CCFF - One study on innovative finance - One impact report for green investment projects

The Integrated National Financing Framework (INFF) and the Blue Financing Strategic Document were launched in 2022. Those, together with the Climate Change Fiscal Framework, which is awaiting endorsement, have enabled the CO to support GOI to systematically scan the financing landscape for the SDGs, blue economy, and climate agenda and analyze forward-looking financing strategies. The development of the carbon tax policy was an equally important factor of the financial system, considering that meeting the NDCs is interlinked with other financing and investment needs. Likewise, through the Climate Finance Network, the CO has also started exploring the development of regional Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) Reference Group to inform the approach to climate budgeting and planning, and mobilization of innovative climate finance. The CO further supported the GOI to strengthen public finance management (PFM) through the development of a carbon tax roadmap study and ESG framework of PPP projects. Concretely, the CO helped the GOI to leverage alternative financing resources for the SDGs, whereby $1.5 billion from the 5th global green Sukuk was raised, and the Indonesia Impact Fund (IIF) deployed USD1 million equity investment to 2 SDG-oriented startups. Furthermore, the CO supported the first SDG bond impact report where EUR500 million was disbursed for health, education, and ICT sectors all of which are contributing to closing the $ 4.7 trillion dollar financing gap.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1. Number of national and/or provincial-level policy and regulatory changes to mainstreaming of forest ecosystem service and biodiversity considerations into national and provincial policies and decision-making processes for forest area planning and management. 2. Tons of CO2e emissions avoided within the three Kalimantan provinces. 3. Area of High Conservation Value (HCV) forests located within the four demonstration landscapes and currently classified as either APL or convertible forest reclassified and/or subject to new and enforceable regulatory protections. 4. Incentive mechanisms in place and operational - to drive changes that significantly reduce the long-term threat or actual incidence of estate-crop driven deforestation. 5. Documented examples of specific lessons shared and applied in other sub-national and national situations.

comment.narrative: 1. Review and/or development of following policies and regulations have not been conducted: (1) regulations to oversight of high biodiversity multiple-use forest landscapes, (2) national and provincial concession-granting processes, (3) regulations governing land classification, including “abandoned lands” regulations, (4) establishment of a mechanism to promote / incentivize use of degraded lands by estate crops sector. 2. There is no additional tons of CO2e can be avoided from selected pilot sites. 3. Forested APL, including HCV areas, has few enforceable or enforced legal or regulatory protections and is therefore subject to high levels of conversion. Therefore, baseline of targeted HCV areas will be established in first year of project implementation. 4. There is few of proven schemes in place to reduce deforestation associated with the estate crops sector. 5. There is no well documented example to Increase knowledge and understanding of the multiple factors underlying successful implementation of reduced deforestation, green growth strategies for Indonesia’s estate crops sector.
All district pilots have their accessible up-to-date and detail baseline data and spatial maps of forests out site state-owned forest, innovative incentives mechanism are tested.

Statistic book of forest outside stated owned forest for Kalimantan was published. UNDP contributed to the publishing the book. The availability of the statistic book has allowed the district governments revisited their spatial planning for better land use arrangement. Village pilots implemented direct incentives in a form of producing immune boosters to be distributed free to medical staffs during COVID-19 pandemic. UNDP contributed to the COVID-19 actions on the field. Ecological Fiscal Transfer mechanism is in ongoing development the ecological fiscal transfer mechanism. UNDP has contributed to the raise of green incentives mechanism to local government to save 197,000 Hectares forest.

- Mid-Term Review - Update baseline spatial maps forest in APL in all pilots district - Non Forest Timber Product identification in Kutai Timur and Sintang - Faciliatation on regulation for forested APL protection

- MidTerm Review report and response completed - Update baseline spatial maps forest in APL in all pilots district completed - Non Forest Timber Product identification in Kutai Timur and Sintang completed - Faciliatation on regulation for forested APL protection in Sintang and East Kalimantan completed
- Facilitation cluster 2 in all pilots districts, 3 vilages per districts identified - LVGA for local champion - Economic Valuation - Assessment two new districts (Sanggau and Kutai Kartanegara)

1. The total area of APL (Land allocated for any non-forest purpose (also known as non-forest area)) forest under enhanced protection in the 3 provinces, namely East Kalimantan, Central Kalimantan and Wes Kalimantan is 663.777 ha, surpassing the end-target of 500.000 ha by nearly 30 percent. 2. UNDP helped the provincial government in East Kalimantan, Central Kalimantan and West Kalimantan through data analysis, expert and multistakeholder discussions, to draft to 11 national or provincial instruction or decrees supporting the conversation of APL (Land allocated for any non-forest purpose (also known as non-forest area)), and in which bioiversity and ecosystem services have been mainstreamed to an extent. These regulations has impacted to 33,58 mTons CO2eq have been avoided based at district level. This figure represents 33.58 mTons CO2eq from avoided deforestation (92,580 ha). 3. Partnerships, UNDP’s engagement of stakeholders in Forest Area Planning & Management in Kalimanta has been strong, and it has in fact been a key part of UNDP’s approach in its work at the provincial and district levels. It has aimed to adapt to local government preferences regarding APL forest management to identify conservation opportunities and have subnational officials take ownership of the achievements. For every regulation drafted with technical support from UNDP, there were several consultations with stakeholders conducted through the process to gain the greatest consensus possible.

a) 3 PLJ data innovations introduced to line ministries, government agencies, or development agencies b) 1 of PLJ innovations adopted by line ministries, government agencies, or development agencies c) 2 contribution to awareness building, capacity, and ecosystem in data innovation in the Asia Pacific region d) 4 requests for collaborations, nationally and internationally e) 5 requests for technical inputs, nationally and internationally f) 2 academic papers/presentation published or presented at national and international conferences by PLJ g) 50 public servants trained in data analytics h) 3 new partnerships in data innovation for development and humanitarian work (including the private sector)

a) 3 PLJ data innovations introduced to Bappenas, Ministry of Foreign Affairs, West Java Provincial Government
b) 1 of PLJ innovations adopted by West Java Provincial Government adopted the interactive map
c) Multiple numbers of contribution to awareness building, capacity, and ecosystem in data innovation in the Asia Pacific region: 1. Participating in FGD about data innovation in National Statistics led by UNFPA and UNRC Cambodia 2. For more awareness building please refer to: https://pulselabjakarta.org/search/media_coverage
d) Requests for collaborations, nationally and internationally: Ministry of Education and Culture Indonesia, Ministry of Finance Indonesia, UN Resident Coordinator Cambodia, UN Women, Price Waterhouse Cooper, Palladium, UNOPS, Facebook Data for Good, International Development Research Center (IDRC), The Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organization (CSIRO), Empatika Consultindo Mandiri
e) 6 technical inputs, nationally and internationally, provided: FGD with UNFPA regional and UNRC Cambodia, input on baselining methodology for PT Kereta Api Indonesia and Bappenas, input for Ministry of Foreign Affairs on how to develop and operate a sustainable digital dashboard, technical input for KOPERNIK on community air quality monitoring, technical input on the usage of conventional dan unconventional datasets to West Java Provincial Government, input on positive deviance method in agriculture to GIZ Data Lab Germany.
f) 3 academic papers/presentation published or presented at national and international conferences by PLJ: 1. The paper about resilience in Cambodia using microfinance accepted in the Machine Learning for Developing World (ML4D) workshop and presented in NeuRIPS conference. 2. PLJ’s works in transportation modelling presented in Asia Pacific Transport Forum 2020, organized by ADB. 3. PLJ’s MIND platform presented in Australasian Aid Conference in Cranford School of Public Policy, Australia National University, Canberra.
g) 80 public servants trained in data analytics: 50 Tim Analitika training within Bappenas Pusdatin, 10 public servants in JDS West Java and 10 public servants in Kemenlu
h) At least 7 new partnerships in data innovation for development and humanitarian work (including the private sector): Facebook Data for Good, International Development Research Center (IDRC), The Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organization (CSIRO), PT Kereta Api Indonesia, Empatika Consultindo, UN Women and Gojek, Palladium.

- Finalisation and presentation of the assessment for the implementation of e-PPGBM (MoH Nutrition Information System), in collaboration with UNICEF - Continue providing inputs and support in the development and implementation of UN Data Strategy through leading the LNOB working group - Finalisation of the UN perception survey, a work commissioned by UN RC Indonesia - Continue working with Bappenas on identifying methodology to identify possible scenarios that can happen in MSME data landscape - Identify the needs of moH through data analytics to inform the arts of possible in a systematic process.

- e-PPGBM assessment has been finalised and report are being prepared for presentation in 2022 - Inputs and contribution to the UN Data Strategy has been rendered in a regular basis. LNOB working group has established a regular meeting - The minimum viable product of MSME mapping has been produced and to be tested by Bappenas - Research dive to identify strategy to integrate health data conducted for further deliberation

- Finalization and handover of MSME Analytical Dashboard to Bappenas - Joint dissemination with the Ministry of Health for results of the Health Data Analytics for Managing Pandemics project - Finalization and dissemination of Breaking Growth Barriers for Women Small and Medium Business Owners research with UN Women - Finalization and dissemination of The Future of MSMEs in Indonesia horizon scanning exercise with Bappenas - Finalization and dissemination of ePPGBM (Managing Nutrition Health Data Platform) implementation gap study with UNICEF and the Ministry of Health - Institutional assessment and design of a low carbon analytics unit - Explore scale-up of the analytical partnerships accelerator model into regional level initiative

Output 1.1. Public Institutions are capable of responsive, inclusive, participatory and representative decision-making
This has been evidenced by the adoption of the Interactive COVID-19 vulnerability Map by the West Java Government and it has been made available for public use, also used as the reference for COVID-19 task force weekly update in West Java. To some extent, the tool has been used to help answer some policy questions related to COVID-19.
Another example is the tool for digital diplomacy has been adopted and further used to generate data as a basis for monthly reporting to the Minister of Foreign Affairs.
Output 4.3. Innovative technology solutions adopted for improved public service delivery (Strategic Plan Output 3.1.1)
PLJ’s work with West Java’s provincial government has resulted in a tool that visualizes data relevant to COVID-19 response. In particular, the tool we developed utilized Facebook’s population density data and combined it with other available data in Indonesia (including Susenas) to identify the risk of transmission and local capacity to handle it down to the village level. PLJ continues to work with the provincial government to identify specific pathways of how insights generated from the tool can feed into policy making processes and intervention design.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
0.1: Extent to which legal or policy or institutional frameworks are in place for conservation, sustainable use, and access and benefit sharing of natural resources, biodiversity and ecosystems. (IRRF Output 2.5 indicator 2.5.1)
0.2: Number of direct project beneficiaries: - Number of government agency staff including enforcement officers who improved their knowledge and skills on IWT due to the project (m/f) - Number of local people in project demonstration areas benefiting from engagement in conservation activities, reduced HWC impacts and improved livelihoods (m/f)
0.3: Expert evaluation of IWT annual volume (number of animal specimens – body parts or live animals) in Indonesia based on the WCS IWT database
0.4: Number of individuals of IWT flagship species (Sumatran Tiger, Sumatran Rhinoceros, Sumatran Elephant, Black-crested macaque, Anoa and Babirusa) killed by poachers annually in the 2 project demonstration areas

comment.narrative:
1.) UU5/1990 need to be revised (the current law has not specifically addressed IWT issue; PP7/1999 has not been revised
2.) 4666 wild animals are seized from 34 protected species
Source: Lakip, Gakkum 2016
3.) 2015: Tiger (5 poached); Elephant (7 poached); Rhino (1 poached); Anoa (10 poached), Babirusa (12), Black-crested macaque (~200

The UNDP has effectively contributing to manage the combating illegal wildlife trade (IWT) in the national framework. UNDP has contributed in the development of National Strategy & Action Plan for combating IWT, and Economic Valuation Wildlife legalization process guidance to determine the economic value of animal traded. UNDP strengthened institutional capacity for implementation & enforcement CIWT by supporting the KAP Survey of communication strategy CIWT (with completion due date is 2021). In 2020, there were 530 law enforcement personnel have improved knowledge. UNDP has supported the institution with curriculum and training Modules for handling protected wildlife crime. To response COVID 19 situation, the UNDP also supports virtual training. As example, in collaboration with BP2SDM MoEF, an e-learning training will be prepared , comprises module on illegal wildlife crimes managerial, technical and attitudes.
To scale-up and improved enforcement strategy at key illegal wildlife trade, UNDP facilitated the Repatriation Wildlife from Philippines to Indonesia. Commitment to influence and support the gender inclusive working environment of the law Enforcement Unit has been showed this year by facilitating training of Inspiring Women to forest rangers at Bogani Nani Watabone National Park . Moreover, UNDP also supported the development of published SOP for animal handling, morphology analysis and DNA Forensic .

1. The project continued the efforts to effective implementation of the national strategy for combating illegal wildlife trade (IWT). It has been completed but it is waiting for legalization to ensure effective adoption, planning and budgeting within MoEF and potentially to be referred by other law enforcement agencies. The strategy has been endorsed by the project board.
2. Two bilateral cooperation have been facilitated through repatriation programme of the illegally traded/smuggled Orangutans in Thailand and Malaysia. This was happened as the results of data sharing and join commitment to combat IWT in the region. The project facilitated development of the Android-based mobile application to identify protected wildlife species which will be used by Government officers in seaports and airports. The existing digital-based operation rooms are operational and delivering inputs in planning, budgeting and making more effective CIWT operations. The analysis from cyber patrol team is also useful to prioritize interventions in suspected areas.
3. The project facilitated the capacities of Bakauheuni seaport check point in Lampung province which is a critical hub for IWT from Sumatera to Java island. Similar efforts have been done in Bitung seaport. To keep the significant results on the law enforcement, the project supported relevant officers in developing participative CIWT plan. In addition, a training on basic investigation technique has been conducted for Boganinani national park in North Sulawesi.
4. Several evidences are available to confirm achievement of the knowledge management target. The project has shared the good practices in the Global Wildlife Programme Conference. Gender-related knowledge products has been produced such as training modules, syllabus, and campaign materials. The project successful in organizing series of gender leadership training which received acknowledgement from the Minister of Environment and Forestry. Women forest rangers have also been established to create opportunity for women in combating IWT efforts. Further, the project and IP representative were also actively attended ASEAN working group on CITES.

(1) Effective national framework for managing wildlife trade (review policies/laws, inter-agency taskforce operational); • Reviewed of Government Regulation No.8/1999 concerning utilization of wild plants & animals species, as well as other regulation related Law No. 5/1990 on Conservation of Biological Natural Resources and their Ecosystems • Developed an equal academic paper for formalization of the CIWT NASTRA. • Revisited and obtained approval of the draft report of the Economic Value Assessment of Protected Wildlife to Support Legal Process. • Drafted SOPs related to Preparation of Evidence for IWT Cases. • Drafted Preparation on Technical Guidelines for Implementing Administration Sanctions for the IWT Cases.
(2) Strengthened institutional capacity for implementation & enforcement at the national and international levels; • Organized National workshop on Strengthening Joint Strategies and Capacity Building on Cyber Patrol for all Gakkum units. • Installed of SIAP Gakkum - Software for Intelligence Center. • Species Validation of Mobile Application on Wildlife Identification. • Developed IWT Information Exchange Application with Financial Transaction Report and Analysis Center and Quarantine Agency. • Conducted Law Enforcement Training related to money laundry and illegal wildlife trade • Conducted Human Resources Capacity Building related to the Provision of Expert Judgement (identification of wildlife type and expert certification). • Conducted Animal Handling Training for Law Enforcement Officer related IWT. • Conducted Training for animal handling and management of human-wildlife conflict (HWC).
(3) Scaling-up improved enforcement strategy at key trade ports & connected ecosystem (increasing number cases prosecuted); • K9 Squad Development • Intelligence Training • Portmate Assessment • Strengthened Regional Task Force on CIWT • Strengthened Intelligence Networks on International IWT at National Borders (Indonesia-Malaysia) • Operations of Illegal Wildlife Trade • Study on Camera Trap Result Data and Pulbaket (Data Collection of Materials and Information) related to TSL Handling.
(4) Knowledge Management (at least 3 project lessons learned) • Developed Communication Strategy based on the national KAP Survey baseline. • Developed MLA (Mutual Legal Assistance) Good Practice on CIWT Joint Action (Indonesia and Netherlands) • Participated in the IWT Anti-trafficking Scout Jamboree/Persami Activities. • Monitoring and Evaluation of IWT E-Learning Training. • Strengthened Inspiring Women for forest rangers partners (PIMP) in the Prevention of IWT Hunting and Trafficking through alternative livelihoods. • Gender Mainstreaming Support (Internal Women Law Enforcement Forum) and LHK Gakkum External Facilitators.

1. At the national level, DG Law Enforcement on Environment and Forestry with support from CIWT project has made a policy paper for recommendation of Law No.5/1990 concerning the conservation of the living natural resources and its ecosystem. Some of the recommended subject matter included in the revision such as: application of civil sanctions, application of economic valuation of IWT crimes, and international cooperation related to IWT crimes. In line with this, the project has completed a study of economic value estimation on 25 wildlife species to support legal processes. The economic value of the 25 types of wildlife is used as a reference/standard by law enforcers (police, prosecutors and judges) in determining the ‘replacement value/fines/ compensation’ that must be prosecuted/sued against perpetrators towards wildlife. This is an effort in strengthening law enforcement policies related to wildlife and conservation.
2. At the regional level, the project also continues to facilitate forest protection operations and snare removal operations. This operation also involves various stakeholders (join coordination) to strengthen law enforcement at the main ecosystem level. In terms of strengthening IWTs crime-handling at trade route entrance such as sea ports, an updated portmate assessment has been carried out at Bitung Port and Belawan Port. The purpose of this activity is to assess the increase in the capacity of law enforcement agencies and to update PortMATE data at Belawan Port, along with analysis, reflection and recommendations for improving performance on efforts to combat illegal wildlife trade.
3. The CIWT project has committed to achieve outputs that promote gender equality in a significant and consistent way. Gender mainstreaming in project cycle is expected able to intervene important areas of changes such as policy and guideline framework, relationships with gender stakeholders, women’s and men’s awareness related IWT, including gender equality. The project and its counterpart have engaged women’s participation in law enforcement activities, especially those related to combatting illegal wildlife trade in the project sites.
From July - November 2022, CIWT project has facilitated several gender activities such as:
Capacity Building Training and Formation of Gender Facilitator Coordinators at central and regional level on 20-23 September 2022.
Capacity Building Training for Social Media Team in creating campaigns related to gender and IWT on 17-18 November 2022
These two trainings are follow-ups from modules and teaching materials (facilitated by CIWT last year) related to gender responsive within the DG Law Enforcement on Environment and Forestry, contributed to the increased knowledge about the concept of gender that can be applied in making gender analytical pathways and gender budget statements in government work programs.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Key legislation gaps are addressed by improved IWT legislation documents

description.narrative: 1.1: The following key legislation gaps are addressed by improved IWT legislation documents approved by Government: - Minimum fines and sentences increased to provide deterrent effect; - Non-native endangered species including elephant, rhinoceros, big cat and pangolin species given legal protection - Indonesian protected species list updated to include all CITES Appendix 1 and globally threatened species - Authority of forestry civil investigators improved - Detention/prison evaluation for creating deterrent effect and rehabilitation for criminals. - online trade regulation to address online wildlife trafficking.

comment.narrative: No legislation gaps are addressed
All key gaps incorporated in the issued legislation and be implemented.
All of the 6 gaps (the sub-indicator) have made significant progress in the issuing of legislation

title.narrative: SES Management Plan
SESP

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
- Number of management plans and appropriate measures implemented for rebuilding or protecting fish stocks including alternative management approaches
- Number of targeted communities of fishers have adopted an ecosystem approach to fisheries management
- Reduced fishing pressure
- Improved use of fish gear/techniques

comment.narrative:
• 0 Number of management plans & targeted communities
• Aru, Indonesia: Approx. 775 registered fishing vessels in Aru operating in the red snapper and shrimp fisheries (combined).
• Aru, Indonesia: Approx. 775 registered fishing vessels in Aru operating in the shrimp and red snapper fisheries; 775 vessels (<30 GT) which do not have VMS; 1400 vessels using gillnet gear.

• Improved stakeholder participation at the regional and national (establishment of a Stakeholder Partnership Forum) • Improved inter sectoral coordination at the national and local levels • Harmonization of national and local policy in Indonesia • Ecosystem approach to fisheries management (EAFM) assessment • Development of profiles of 3 fisheries in the ATSEA, value chain analysis and pre-assessment • Support strengthening of regional and national actions for Responsible Fishing Practices Including Combating IUU Fishing • Strengthening the capacity of stakeholder in NTT to respone marine pollution • Updated information and database on coral, mangrove and seagrass beds in the ATS • Establish new MPA in Kolepom and Improve management effectiveness in SAP southeast Aru • Implementation of Integrated Coastal Management

In 2020, the project established the management body for FMA 718, engaged bio-economy assessment for shrimp as basis information of sea management at FMA 718, updated Fisheries Management plan for FMA 718 to harmonized national and local policy. The EAFM strategy and action for management guidance was developed and EAFM assessment for red snapper in Aru district are now available with the aggregate status from 6 domains is yellow to green. The project concluded the EAFM assessment for shrimp fisheries in Aru district with recommendation on aggregate status from 6 domains is red to green. Fisheries profile for Red snapper, which provides the information on fisheries characteristic, status of population, value chain and MSC’s pre-Assessment. Moreover, Initial report of shrimp fisheries profile in FMA 718 were developed, which provide the information on stock status and production. On capacity building, the support to the initiation of forum for prosecution of fisheries violations in Papua province was given. Capacity building are designed and planned to support pilot study of data science and management for fisheries surveillance in FMA 718. Project also facilitated the the initiation of marine pollution task force for NTT, as well as support the development of action plan document for this task force, and updated status on the health of marine resources based on remote sensing and field work (biophysical survey).
New MPA initiation in Kolepom island were developed that cover 350,000 Ha ( Governor Decree Papua No.188.4/295/2019) 350,000 Ha. In 2020, project contributed in increasing status of management effectiveness of existing MPA in SAP Southeast Aru, from 58% to 68% since the intervention from ATSEA-2. Report progress on climate change assessment and marine pollution in the regional include in Rote Ndao district were developed.
All activities in 2020, were contributed to the strengthened marine management for target fisheries.

1. Establishment of the NIMC Indonesia 2. Technical support for the operationalization of the Fisheries Management Body (LPP) of the Fisheries Management Area (WPP) 718. 3. Establishment of the Provincial Marine Pollution Task Force in NTT province. 4. Initiation of the collaboration with Musamus University in Merauke district, Papua province to establish Papua regions EAFM Learning center. 5. Development of the Kolepom MPA Zonation plan. 6. Development of the Early warning system tool for oil spill in Timor seas. 7. Improved facilities of the science lab for better managing fisheries stock data.

1. The draft of National Inter Ministerial Committees (NIMCs) concept has been developed as the output of series of consultation in 2021. The legal assessment to support the formalization process of the NIMCs has been established, which comprises the legal structure options, operational plan, and roles and responsibilities of the members. The NIMCs model design will be aligned with the SPF structure which is under development. Additionally, the NIMCs institutional mapping and programs from each ministry that could contribute to the implementation of the ATSEA-2 NAP has been made available. 2. ATSEA-2 supported the process of updating the Operational guidelines of the WPPNRI LPP 718 in collaboration with local stakeholders who worked in WPP 718. The NCU is part of the partnership working group led by the Maluku Provincial Authority in collaboration with other CSOs. 3. The Marine Pollution Task Force in NTT Province has been established and formalized through a governor decree of NTT No.260/2021. The team has already established the action plan to strengthening the marine pollution handling through some strategies, such as strengthening the database, developing a monitoring and reporting mechanism, capacity building, and developing a well-established network with community and other stakeholder. 4. The establishment of EAFM learning Center is still currently underway. Some initial process which has been completed include capacity building of the lecturer, local government, and other local stakeholder through EAFM training that have been supported by LC EAFM from Pattimura University. Technical skills related to utilization of the EAFM tools have been improved by 47,5% in average from 11 participant. Additionally, knowledge sharing mechanism has been established to support LC EAFM in Papua. 5. Draft zonation plan of Kolepom Island has been developed. Three scenarios have been discussed internally by the zonation working group that have been formalized through SK Sekda Papua No.188.4/21536/SET in 2021. The conservation target has been decided to conserve estuarine area, demersal fisheries, and sawfish. The public consultation will be held in 2022 6. The development of early warning system is currently underway. It is now in the stage of recruitment process (the bidder’s selection). The main objective of the recruitment is to develop an integrated monitoring and reporting system to support quick respond on situation related to marine pollution, to develop an oil spill contingency plan and to provide conceptual model of revolving fund mechanism to manage the oil spill in Timor Seas, East Nusa Tenggara Provincial. 7. The plan to establish an improved facilities of the science lab for better managing fisheries stock data has been cancelled. This is due to the restructuring process of government agencies, which has affected the authorities and responsibilities related to the respective agencies. The transition of Pusriskan to BRIN has changed the authority and resources availability related to the fisheries database management, namely from MMAF to BRIN.

1. Developed the legalization of NIMCs and SPF 2. Developed Fisheries database platform in Maluku Province to support Sustainable fisheries 3. Established the IUU fisheries Forum in Maluku district to support surveillance in Fisheries Management Plan 718 4. Developed a tool for Early warning System of marine pollution mechanism which is led by NTT provincial government 5. Established of MPA Kolepom in Merauke District through Ministerial decree of MMAF 6. Established Gender mainstreaming Working Group (PUG) in Rote Ndao and Aru district to support implemention of the Gender action plan.

ATSEA-2 Project has supported the Government of Papua Province and Merauke Regency to establish a conservation zone in Papua Province, especially in Kolepom Island waters of Merauke Regency. An official letter from the Governor of Papua Number 522.1/11677/SET on 27 September 2022 has been submitted to the Minister of Marine Affairs and Fisheries requesting an official approval through the establishment of Marine Conservation Zone in Kolepom Island waters. Further decision will be in the form of the Ministerial Decree.
Furthermore, In this period, there are no concrete results yet from the implementation of this project, as the processes towards implementing sustainable fisheries management, establishment of Marine Protected Area (MPA), and resilience to climate change and oil spill in the ATS region are still ongoing.
One key assessment result was obtained in this period that would enable future activities to reach the desired outcomes, namely the results from Red Snapper EAFM implementation in WPP 718 that was prepared with the support of YKAN. Also, ATSEA-2 with the support from DFW was able to develop and finalize a field monitoring guideline that can be used by local community surveillance groups (Pokmaswas) in remote locations.
NCU-ID ATSEA-2 has facilitated the second training on gender mainstream advocacy in Rote Ndao Regency, NTT Province on 25-27 July 2022. The training was conducted in Hotel Anugerah Nembrala, Rote Ndao Regency and attended by Vice Head of Rote Ndao Regency (NTT Province), officials from Ministry of Marine and Fisheries, Head of Women Empowerment and Child Protection, Rote Ndao Regency (NTT Province), Bapelitbangda NTT Province, Women Empowerment and Child Protection Kupang (NTT Province), and Inspectorate of Rote Ndao Regency (NTT Province). The training has resulted in the following recommendation:
It is essential for Rote Ndao Government (PPRG driver institutions) to learn from more advanced region in gender mainstreaming implementation in order to establish gender focal point and gender working group as well as to develop local regulation that will strengthen gender mainstreaming implementation in Rote Ndao Regency;
Discussion with MMAF on the feasibility to have further coordination related to seaweed village program to help women seaweed grower industry;
ATSEA-2 will facilitate the development and enactment of gender focal point decree that shall be conducted in upcoming months.
s part of gender action plan in Rote Ndao, one of the objective is strengthened gender mainstreaming implementation at local government level in 17 person from Rote Ndao District and NTT province. The improved understanding on gender aspects and how to incorporate them into strategic planning are they were also able to develop gender mainstreaming plans for their respective agencies/organizations.
In community level the access of resources for womens are assist with strengthening 3 women’s business group in Boa, Oeseli, and Daiama village that utilize and manage seawood, mangrove and coconut tree product as their alternative livelihood.
Strategic discussions, followed by training/workshop on Gender Mainstreaming involving 17 participants (14 female and 3 male) from NTT provincial government, Rote Ndao local government, as well as CSO partners in NTT and Rote Ndao was held on 14-16 June 2022 in Ba’a, Rote Ndao. As a result of this activity, the participants gained an improved understanding on gender aspects and how to incorporate them into strategic planning, they were also able to develop gender mainstreaming plans for their respective agencies/organizations.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

comment.narrative: Project preparation , including identification of supports and opportunity and risk are calculated as preparation for the full implementation until 2023. All advocacy and assistance are started for the implementation
Project documents are approved, and activities with government and stakeholders are implemented on track

Outcome 1: 1.1.1 Identification, design and establishment of an Institute to support energy efficient lighting transformation in Indonesia 1.1.2 Periodic (inception, mid-term and terminal) gender sensitive market surveys of EEL production and application in Indonesia 1.1.3 Development of LED lighting development roadmap for Indonesia, with gender equality goals included & 1.1.4 Review and development of recommendations for government policies to provide level playing field to manufacturing of high quality LEDs
1.2 Adopted and implemented business transformation plans of selected local lighting manufacturers to produce high quality energy efficient lighting which meet future MEPS
1.3.1 Design, planning and conduct of training for financing institution officials on assessment of financial proposals on energy efficiency, focusing on production upgrades of lighting manufacturers, while specifically addressing gender-gaps in the market.
Outcome 3: 3.1.1 & 3.1.2 Consulting services regarding the Study on Comparative Evaluation of International Best Practices of Financial Support Schemes for EEL financing 3.1.3: Mainstreaming of the existing national policies and regulations for the banking sector to accelerate penetration of EEL. 3.2.2 Design, approval, capacity development and implementation of pilot demonstrations using ESCO business models in street lighting, buildings and residences 3.2.2: Design, planning and conduct of training for local government municipalities and cities and pilot demonstration hosts 3.3.1: Development of a gender-responsive EEL awareness and promotional program on the benefits of good quality LED lighting

Outcome 1: 1.1.1. The Efficient Lighting Knowledge Center established under the EBTKE with new features that enables knowledge exchange between key stakeholders (LED producers, regulators, etc) 1.1.2. Surveys local LED industries (both online or site visits) completed with total number of the industries surveyed is 26 industries. Results has been presented to various stakeholders. 1.1.3. The Roadmap for the Development of Local LED Industry finalized.
1.2. Development of a business transformation plan for selected local LED lighting industries carried out.
1.3.1. the training module for financing institutions official regarding the assessment of bankable proposals from local LED industries in order to increase production efficiency and quality, business expansion, and other business development goals developed.
Outcome 3: 3.1.1 & 3.1.2. Consulting services to conduct the Study on Comparative Evaluation of International Best Practices of Financial Support Schemes for EEL financing selected. 3.1.3. Financial support instruments or schemes identified. 3.2.2.Wonosobo and Lombok Barat districts as well as Banjarmasin city signed MoU with Directorate of Energy Conservation to be pilot project of streetlighting. 3.3.1. Gender-responsive EEL awareness and promotional program on the benefits of good quality LED lighting conducted.

Outcome 1 1.1.1 Microsite containing the results of industrial surveys and introduction of domestic LED lighting industry products, and Promotion and socialization of the domestic LED lighting industry held in various regions (west, middle and east) 2 times 1.1.2 Results of consumer and market surveys (retail) 1.1.3 & 1.1.4 Dissemination of results roadmap 1.2.1 & 1.2.2 & 1.2.3 Domestic LED lighting industry can make bankable and get financing from banks 1.3.1 Training module, and Training to financial service institutions 2 times
Outcome 3 3.1.1 & 3.1.2 Recommendations for Financial Support Schemes for LED Project Financing for Indonesia in various sub-sectors, including recommendations for an ESCO Business Model suitable for Indonesia and the ADLIGHT Pilot Project 3.1.3 Preparation of procedures, implementation of three national banking forums, dissemination of project results 3.2.1 Implementation of energy conservation pilot projects in APJ in 6 districts/cities, and Implementation of energy conservation pilot projects in the lighting sector in 1 government building and 1 commercial building 3.2.2 Increased capacity of district/city governments & energy users/hosts) in energy management and procurement systems with innovative financing/business models 3.3.1 & 3.2.2 Strengthening education and raising public awareness regarding EEL for various groups

1. The approval of the Ministerial Decree for minimum energy performance standards (MEPS) and energy labels for light emitting diode (LED) lights in July 2022 marks a significant milestone for Indonesia’s ADLIGHT, Advancing Indonesia’s Lighting Market to High Efficient Technologies, project. The project is funded by the Global Environment Facility (GEF) and implemented by United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and UNDP. 2.. To that end, a GEF-financed project called ADLIGHT is helping Indonesia transition to more energy-efficient lighting technologies, a process that could end up saving up to 250,000 tons of CO2 eq in greenhouse gas emissions. Through ADLIGHT, the UN produced a roadmap on developing the Light-Emitting Diode (LED) industry in Indonesia, designed to help local LED producers become more competitive in the domestic market. Of 23 cities and districts scoped for interest and potential mass utilization of high-efficient LED lights, two have since signed MOUs with the Directorate of Energy Conservation, Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources, to move ahead with baseline studies. 3. The survey of local LED industry have been completed and the Roadmap for Development of LED industry in Indonesia is finalized. The Roadmap provides direction for local LED industry to become more competitive and increase their market share, especially in the domestic market (green growth objective of the CPD). 4. With regards to the target of 13,960 tCO2eq direct GHG emissions, UNDP through GEF-financed project has implemented The streetlighting in Palu and LED lighting in public buildings in Semarang, West Lombok, and Palu, while 3 other City/Regency namely Banda Aceh, Banjarmasin and Wonosobo for streetlighting pilot demonstration and LED lighting pilot demonstration in public building in Semarang City will be implemented in Q3 and Q4 in the year 2022

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 181.76 ODP tonnes consumption on HCFCs in Indonesia

comment.narrative: 363.51 ODP tonnes consumption on HCFCs in Indonesia

Capacity building including training for custom officer and service technican. Development of training material and Indonesian Work Qualification (KKNI) Scheme for service technician is in place and awareness regarding mandatory certification for RAC technician. Distribution of 2R/3R for 50 service technician

There were 271 customs officer trained in 6 port of Import in Indonesia (including 2 in borders area). Due to covid-19 120 Air Conditioning service technician trained for level-1 based on Indonesia Qualification Framework number P.73 year 2019 using online training and awareness for mandatory professional certification conducted using webminar in cooperation with Ministry of Manpower, ITB, Gabel and ITSB. Scheme for professional certification of RAC technician will be finalised in Q3. The activities conducted in 2020, has progress the works of UNDP in the RAC sector, in strengthening the capacities of relevant industries and other stakeholders to contribute for the reduction of HCFC.

Capacity building including training for custom officer for ODS import control and identification of refrigerant. Training and competency certification for service technican and also for instructor in training institution for the implementation of SKKNI number 41 Year 2019 and KKNI number P.73 Year 2019. Development of training material and Indonesian Work Qualification (KKNI) Scheme for service technician is in place and awareness regarding mandatory certification for RAC technician. Distribution of 2R/3R for 50 service technician and 6 training institution. And also identification of 9 training institution for RAC training program.

RAC (Project ID 00101883) Result 2021 1. Trained 27 custom officer in Dumai port in Riau for HCFCs import control and the use of refrigerant identifier to identified ODS and non-ODS refrigerant. 2. Trained 62 instructor in training institution for the implementation of SKKNI number 41 Year 2019. 3. Developed 5 Training Curriculum for service Technician for good servicing practices in refrigeration and AC for Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4 and Level 5. The 5 training curricula are issued through a decree from Head of Education and Training Centre, MOEF. 4. Developed 12 scheme for service technician competency certification. The twelve schemes were endorsed by MOEF and BNSP. 5. Distribution of 50 set equipment to service technician and 6 training institution 6. Distribution of 1 set mini reclaim to training centre 7. Identified 9 training centre to apply good servicing training for RAC technician 8. Dissemination of HCFC phase-out program and mandatory certification for service technician to 760 participant

Target 2022 1. Training and certification for 500 AC Technician 2. Training and certification for 18 instructor for refrigeration and AC 3. Training for custom officer in 4 location 4. Distribution 27 set equipment to RAC technician and 15 vocational training center

1. UNDP and Ministry of Environment and Forestry through Montreal Protocol provided Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) trainers and technicians with good servicing practice training focusing on RAC installation, service and troubleshooting. In 2022, 633 RAC technicians and 54 RAC trainers across Indonesia gained benefits from a series of training to cope up safety aspects while handling HCFCs and newly introduced refrigerants which have higher pressure and flammability compared to HCFCs. These HCFCs contain ozone depleting potential (ODP) and global warming potential (GWP). The training is meant to assure health and safety when the technicians are working in designated locations (houses, buildings, etc). 2. Through the HPMP Stage II project, UNDP facilitated technical support to the Ministry of Environment and Forestry to develop policy and relevant technical guidance to phase-out of HCFC-141b consumption that have been included and enacted under Ministerial Decree of Trade Import Regulation number 18 Year 2021 on goods prohibited for export and goods prohibited for import. The policy and technical guidance are important to protect Indonesia from the importation of products banned by the protocol and assure the health and safety of Indonesian from the danger of HCFCs. UNDP facilitated Indonesia to comply with the schedule of the Montreal Protocol. 3. UNDP facilitated the procurement and distribution of RAC machines and equipment to designated vocational training centres across Indonesia in close coordination with the Ministry of Environment and Forestry and Ministry of Manpower. Through the distribution of these machines and equipment, the training centres are expected to adhere to updated RAC technology being introduced and adopted by the global community. IS: 1. UNDP has facilitated the Government of Indonesia in the ratification process of the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol in 2022. The Presidential Regulation Number 129 of 2022 on the Ratification of the Amendment to the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, Kigali, 2016 has been signed by the President on 1 November 2022. Then, on 14 December 2022, the ratification instrument was accepted by the UN depositary. As the amendment is ratified, the Government of Indonesia will start the implementation of the HFC Phase-down in Indonesia starting on 14 March 2023, 90 days after the entry into force. 2. UNDP has been working closely with the Ministry of Environment and Forestry to develop and establish regulatory frameworks able to control the importation and distribution of refrigerants consumed by Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) installers, manufacturers, and servicing workshops. The project facilitated the release of Ministry of Trade Regulation Number 25/ Year 2022 on the Importation Policy and Regulation of Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS). 3. UNDP facilitated the Indonesia government to collect, review and report ODS data to Global Ozone Secretariat at timely and effective manner (annually) in compliance with the Montreal Protocol. UNDP facilitated the Government of Indonesia to actively join international meetings, South-South Cooperation and technical discussions. Besides UNDP facilitated International Day of Ozone Layer Protection on 16 September 2022. 4. For the import control of HCFC to Indonesia, HPMP stage II project support capacity building of custom officer through training of ODS import control and indentification of refrigerant. In 2022, trainig for custom has been conducted in 4 location mainly in the border area to prevent the illegal import of control substances

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: 1. The availability of adaptation Taskforce coordination strategy. 2. The academic study report of the RAN-API status. 3. Legal standing for the RAN-API. 4. Technical guidelines on RAN-API implementation, monitoring and evaluation. 5. The number of delivered training packages on gender-responsive budget tagging for CCA priorities. 6. The amount of gender-responsive budget tagging for CCA investment priorities.
Outcome 2: 1. The number of validated indicators for CCA priorities identified in the NDC. 2. The number of training materials for climate risk assessments for CCA priorities identified in the NDC. 3. The number of government officials, civil society representatives and academia that took part in the training for climate risk assessments.
Outcome 3: 1. Sum of stakeholders took part in multi-stakeholder consultation assessing capacity needs for climate risk assessments for landscape-based adaptation in Wakatobi. Participants will include selected task force members, the Ministry of Women’s Empowerment and Child Protection, BKF and local government and non-government stakeholders (civil society, private sector, academia) from Wakatobi. 2. The availability of capacity needs assessment report. 3. The number of developed capacity-building packages for climate risk assessments for landscape-based adaptation approaches. 4. Number of participants took part in the training of trainers (ToT) workshops on conducting vulnerability and risk assessments and identifying landscape-based adaptation options in Wakatobi. Participants will include selected task force members, the Ministry of Women’s Empowerment and Child Protection, BKF and local government and non-government stakeholders (civil society, private sector, academia) from Wakatobi. 5. Number of local government and non-government stakeholders (civil society, private sector, academia) from Wakatobi takes part in training for vulnerable communities related to climate risk and impact assessments. 6. Sum

comment.narrative:
1.1 Adaptation Taskforce exists, but needs revitalising and strengthening, with coordination mechanisms enhanced 1.2 Legal standing for RAN-API does not currently exist to govern adaptation unlike the framework for mitigation 1.3 RAN-API of Indon esia has recently been reviewed but needs updating based on developmental priorities, and recent data for thematic areas 1.4 Indonesia has policies established to highlight the need for gender-responsive CCA, and a climate change budgeting system. But there is a need to merge the two concepts for effective implementation of a gender-responsive CCA budget tagging system at the national level.
2.1 Vulnerability assessment has been conducted by SIDIK at the national level, but the parameters are limited and socio-economic indicators are lacking. Gender data is not incorporated into the risk assessments 2.2 Scientific information that can inform indicators is limited. Currently there is only a prototype for the resilience index rather than an actual index 2.3 Climate risk assessment and gender-responsive risk and vulnerability assessments are relatively new concepts for which capacity is not sufficiently prevalent. Stakeholders do not have the skills and technical expertise necessary to conduct gender-responsive climate risk and vulnerability assessments.
3.1 Climate risk assessment is a new concept which was introduced through the IPCC AR5 report, and which has not yet been implemented by many stakeholders in Indonesia. Among government staff and other stakeholders, capacity for developing risk assessments for climate change impacts is limited. 3.2 For Wakatobi there are no gender-responsive climate risk assessments that have been conducted using landscape-based adaptation approaches 3.3 Climate budgeting has been introduced in Indonesia for CCA at the national level, but sub-national level CCA budgeting is not practiced. 3.4 There is no gender-responsive budget tagging system for CCA in Wakatobi
Not available as we are in the state of Prodoc Development.
Not available as we are in the state of Prodoc Development.

Outcome 1: 1. Adaptation Taskforce strengthened, and ToR developed, with roles, responsibilities and coordination mechanisms determined. 2. RAN-API updated and validated, including approaches for institutional strengthening and cross-sectoral coordination, the establishment of an M&E framework and an analysis of climate risk and vulnerability assessment indicators and impact assessment methodologies for adaptation options.
Outcome 2 1. SIDIK improved and indicators for climate risk and vulnerability developed. 2. Climate risk indicators/indices for the development of a RAN-API M&E framework reviewed and determined and impact assessment methodologies for CCA options defined.
Outcome 3 1. Gender-responsive climate risk assessments conducted in Wakatobi utilizing a landscape-based adaptation approach 2. Capacity of government staff in Wakatobi built on gender-responsive budget tagging for CCA and gender-responsive climate budget tagging guidelines developed.
The project is still accommodating input from stakeholders. It will be PAC in early 2023

comment.narrative: 2.1 SMILE Scale-up on 600 health centers; techno-economic assessment of SMILE; Preliminary report on interoperability from SMILE to MOH e-logistic DHIS-2. 2.2 PRs progress report reflecting improvement in managing ATM programs and finance management. Gender perspective will be incorporated in this report. 2.3 Analysis on implementation of Roadmap of Hazardous and toxic substance management at health services facility; Pilot project in 2 districts for climate change adaptation, including air pollution analysis

The SMILE has been scaled up to selected provinces and regions (Riau, Gorontalo, Jateng, Jabar, Jakarta, Banten) , involving 600 health centers, and has gathered full supports from MoH. Improvement result in managing ATM Program and finance and human resources management was achieved in 2020, includes the additional supports on implementation of Tuberculosis Multi Drugs Resistance (TB-MDR) clinic renovation in more than 40 hospitals. In 2020, the assessment on mainstreaming human rights and gender in HIV/AIDS has been started and will continue until early 2021, to support inputs to policy implementation.

SMILE - 1.800 health facilities in routine immunization, 10,000 health facilities in COVID19 and providing 3.000 remote temperature logger - continue support government of Indonesia in supply chain management and logistic monitoring for vaccine, including covid-19 vaccination MTC: Project Support for strengthen the function of CCM Indonesia to ensure programme implementation performance through providing technical and management support

SMILE - 6000 health facilities in routine immunization and more than 10,000 health facilities (Primary Health Centre and Hospitals) in COVID19 are using the SMILE online logistic monitoring and 3.000 remote temperature logger are installed - MoH approved plan to expand the Smile Platform to be use as an online stock inventory and monitoring system for medicines and non medicinal product (reagents). MTC: Successfully support the development of HIV country proposal grant for year 2022 and Health System Strengthening stream for 2022-2023 implementation

SMILE - 7000 health facilities in routine immunization and maintained 10,000 health facilities (Primary Health Centre and Hospitals) in COVID19 are using the SMILE online logistic monitoring and 4.000 remote temperature logger procurement - Medical supply chain management assessment and development of national online stock inventory and monitoring system for medicines and non medicinal product (reagents).

S2 Progress - 1920 loggers will be installed in December

description.narrative: Implementation for technical suport for grant management and support for the CCM Indonesia full compliance for implementation development or improvement The Global Fund project requirement

comment.narrative: technical assistance and support for CCM Secretariat Indonesia to support AIDS, TB, and Malaria Programme.
Not yet started
Not yet started

- ensure the Global Fund implementation in which PRs have to fullfil the Global Fund requirement such as the PRs Project Implementation Manual (PIM) development or improvement, monev etc. - ensure providing technical assistance for grant management and support oversight functions of the CCM Indonesia through Technical Working Group for AIDS, TB, and Malaria

1. Preparation of Capacity Assessment After being announced as a PR candidate by the CCM, the new TB PR Community – Consortium of Penabulu STPI, is preparing to enter the GFATM capacity assessment which was conducted by the Local Fund Agency of the GFATM. Before signing a grant agreement with a Principal Recipient, the Global Fund ensures that the proposed implementation arrangements are sound. To do so, the Global Fund assesses whether systems and capacities of grant implementers are adequate for effective management of the grant funds. This is required to ensure the Global Fund partnership with the country can deliver maximum impact against the three diseases. The assessment is done using a tool called the Capacity Assessment tool. The UNDP technical advisor provides support by introducing the capacity assessment tools that will be used by the LFA, and by assisting the new PR to prepare – which includes their knowledge and understanding of country proposals that have been approved by the GFATM’s TRP.
2. Support during the CAT The UNDP technical advisor provides support to the new PR during an assessment to ensure that the new PR could properly present that the proposed implementation arrangements, systems and capacities of key grant implementers are adequate for effective financial and programmatic management of the grant funds. The UNDP technical advisor also assists and supports a new PR team to adequately respond to LFA questions and clarification in the following functional areas: · monitoring and evaluation · procurement and supply management · financial management and systems · governance and program management (including Sub-recipient management)
3. Support during the Grant Making Process During the grant-making process, the Country Coordinating Mechanism (CCM) and the country team of the Global Fund work with the PR of the documents required for a preparation of the Grant Confirmation. The UNDP technical advisor facilitates and supports the new PR to understand the process and to prepare the entire documents specific for a new PR, as a part of the Funding request documents. They are Performance Framework (PF), Work plan and detail budget, PSCM and M&E
4. Develop M&E plan The UNDP technical advisor assists and technically supports the PR Consortium of PB-STPI to develop this plan, to ensure that the new PR have a monitoring and evaluation plan based on the agreed PF document. The monitoring and evaluation plan contain detailed information on: · Indicators · Data management · Data quality assurance · Evaluations · Monitoring and evaluation coordination · Capacity building for monitoring and evaluation · Budget and workplan for monitoring and evaluation.
The monitoring and evaluation plan should also include activities and budget to strengthen the national M&E system. The M&E plan must be well developed and submitted prior to the grant signing, and must also be in line with the National M&E plan developed by the National program of the Ministry of Health.
5. Develop Project Implementation Manual This document is required and is one of the important documents that must be developed by every PR as a guideline for the implementation/operational activities in accordance with the work plan and budget to achieve the target according to the indicators in the PF document that have been determined and agreed between PR and CT.
Each Project Implementation Manual should at least describe the organization of the PR, SR, and SSR grant management. This organization was developed to accommodate the Implementation Arrangements Mapping which had also been mutually agreed upon. The next chapter that must be written is the implementation of activities to achieve the targets according to the indicators in the PF, followed by a monitoring and evaluation system for the implementation of this program. Financial management & procurement and supply management should also be written very clearly in this guide.
6. Technical Assistance for new PRs on Institutional Strengthening in Managing Grant GFATM. This includes technical input for workshop PIM for the PR team, Monthly meeting PR and the GFATM Country Team, Quarterly Review Meeting PRs, TWG and The GF Country team, COVID-19 Response Mechanism (C19RM) proposal development, and add Debt-to-Health Swap financed interventions to TB grants.
7. Assessment Report of Aids, Tb, Malaria Supply Chain Management
8. Assessment Report of Gender Mainstreaming on Policy and Program related to HIV and AIDS Prevention and Program

- Strengthen the CCM Indonesia in managing multi-donor funds and facilitating CCM Indonesia’s activities - Management and Technical Assistance for a new PRs/National Sub-Recipients (SRs) - Strengthening implementation of community, human rights, gender - Technical Assistance on local government’s development planning implementation and local government budgeting on prevention and care of AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria program

S1 Progress: - 7 out of 11 CCM has been hired to strengthen function of CCM and TWG ATM through monitoring monthly call meeting - Recruited an advisor to oversee implementation of activities and development of report of 2 new PR (Adinkes and Consortium STPI Penabulu) - Recruited a Human Right Technical Officer for a strategized implementation of cross-cutting activities - Recruited technical officer for RSSH S2 progress: - Facilitating TWGs activities; Facilitating Field Oversight Visit in 5 priority provinces, including West Java and Raja Ampat - Actively involved in CCM and Technical Working Group of each components (Aids, TB, Malaria, and RSSH) by proving input, advice, endorsement, and evaluate PR Performance - High level technical support will be provided by dr. Carmelia Basri until December 2022 as project advisor Together with UNAIDS and UNWomen, Strengthen feedback mechanisms from community, focused on human rights and gender issues to reduce stigma and discrimination of key populations. - With UNAIDS and UNWomen, Strengthen rights-based and gender transformative approaches in national response especially supported by GFATM through provision of technical assistance for Global Fund Principal Recipients - Strengthens supply chain management for ARV and Non- ARV in 2 recommended provinces

description.narrative: 3.1. SP4N-LAPOR! users at the end of the project (2022) compared to the baseline year increased by 50%. 3.2. Citizen’s satisfaction rate towards SPAN-LAPOR! system and handling agencies at the end of the project (2022) is 70%. 3.3. 50% of the participants attending the SPAN-LAPOR! public workshop are women

comment.narrative: 3.1. As of 2018, 777.03 total active users for SP4N LAPOR! 3.2. There is no baseline for citizen satisfaction rate 3.3. There is no public workshops on SP4N-LAPOR! conducted by KOICA and UNDP

3.1. SP4N-LAPOR! users compared to the baseline year increased by 20% (approximately 958.453 users). 3.2. Citizen’s satisfaction rate towards SPAN-LAPOR! system is from 60% to 70%. 3.3. 50% of the participants attending the SPAN-LAPOR! public workshop in 2020 are women

3.1. As per the latest data, from Ministry of Administrative and Bureaucratic Reform (KemenPAN-RB) at 15 December 2020, SP4N-LAPOR! users increased by 40% (Approximately 1.188.964 users). 3.2. The citizen satisfaction rate in 2020 will be published at April/May 2021 because the satisfaction survey was delayed due to COVID-19 . 3.3.The COVID 19 pandemic also influence the implementation of the most planned workshops which impact to the postponed to 2021. In 2020, the public discussion and content creator workshop were successfully attended by 50% female participants or more precisely gender balance was achieved, as part of commitment to ensure gender equality in participation.

3.1. SP4N-LAPOR! users compared to the baseline year increased by 40-50% 3.2. Citizen’s satisfaction rate towards SPAN-LAPOR! system is from 70% to 75%. 3.3. Above 40% of the participants attending the SPAN-LAPOR! public workshop in 2020 are women

3.1. As per the latest data, from Ministry of Administrative and Bureaucratic Reform (KemenPAN-RB) at December 2021, SP4N-LAPOR! users increased by 75% (Approximately 1.394.577users). 3.2. Satisfaction rate of 75.7% (up by 3.7%) based on the third generation of SP4N LAPOR! 3.3. On average 50% female participation has been achieved in the large-scale public events including SDGs talk and the annual workshop held in April and the FGD held with sub-national government

3.1. SP4N-LAPOR! users compared to the baseline year increased by 50% as agreed by KemenPAN-RB and KOICA considering the update of the system 3.2. Citizen’s satisfaction rate towards SPAN-LAPOR! system is 75%. 3.3. Above 40% participants in public workshop/outreach are female

3.1. Increased by more than one-hundred percent (100%) of the number of SP4N-LAPOR! users nationwide compared to the baseline year (i.e. 1.707.256 users compared to 798.711 Users as baseline) 3.2. As per the 2022 survey report, the citizen satisfaction rate stands 73.7%. 3.3. 43% female participate in public workshop in 2022
