title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1.1 Number of SDGs Secretariat strengthened, on coordination, planning and budgeting, and monitoring, including gender sensitization at national and sub-national level 1.2 Number of SDGs related research using gender lens conducted, and results published and disseminated 1.3 Number of SDGs data system at national and sub-National level developed or strengthened, sex- disaggregated data system will be applied, wherever available 1.4 Number of universities adopted SDGs mainstreaming 2.1 Number of “Kawasan SDGs” established through broad consultation with all stakeholders and additional funds mobilized by SDGs Secretariat. 2.2 Number of innovative solutions in Kawasan SDG adopted and replicated by other partner/ LGs/ministries 2.3 Number of Knowledge products produced and disseminated including exposure of gender equality targets 2.4 Number of estimated people reached through knowledge products dissemination 2.5 Number of SDGs related capacity building and advocacy events conducted and/ or facilitated at national, regional and global level (at least 50% participants are women) 2.6 Number of agencies/ populations advocated 2.7 Percentage increase in capacity of participant from previous activities

comment.narrative: Activity Result 1.1: SDGs Secretariat strengthened on coordination, planning and budgeting, and monitoring, including gender sensitization at national and sub national level. Activity Result 1.2: SDGs related research using gender lens conducted, and results published and disseminated. Activity Result 1.3: SDGs data system at national and sub-national level developed or strengthened with sex-disaggregated data-basis wherever available. Activity Result 1.4: Mainstreaming of SDGs in University conducted. Activity Result 2.1: “Kawasan SDGs”5 established through broad consultation with all stakeholders and additional funds mobilized by SDGs Secretariat. Activity Result 2.2: Knowledge products produced and disseminated including exposure of gender equality targets through various media platforms. Activity Result 2.3: SDGs related capacity building and advocacy events conducted and/ or facilitated, with equal participation of women and men, at national, regional and global events.

1.Riau Program will be concluded by Q4 of 2020, in doing so will continue to provide technical assistance to related Bappeda counterparts by ensuring community participation to accomplish and initiatives such as integrated SDG data system at provincial level, and rural/urban SDG village initiatives at district level. 2. Implementation of Social Innovation Platform in 2 provinces: Gorontalo and West Java District, in partnership with BRH, MoV and ALC 3. Enhanced works with Kemendesa (will be in a form of Cooperation Letter). 4.Conducting SDGs Debate and Conference annually in partnership with Bappenas and Tanoto Foundation, in the upcoming 2020 Debate and will target to attract 100 Indonesian university teams. UNDP Indonesia will be the main organiser of the 2020 Indonesian SDGs Debate, in close coordination with the Indonesian Student Debate Community as the Convener.

1.Handover to SDG Secretariat of Riau Province: SDGs Data System which has been integrated, One Stop Service application for BASADA, concept of Fire Free Village drafted. 2. The Village Region (Kawasan Pedesaan ) Development towards SDGs 2030, is piloted at target provinces. 3.ACHIEVE will partner together with youth groups and universities.

1. Supporting Pekanbaru city government in implementing SDGs Action through BASADA initiative (website and mobile apps development, advocacy of BASADA adoption into local government existing system) 2. Supporting Government of Indonesia in preparing 2021 SDGs VNR and its communications materials 3. Supporting SDGs knowledge sharing through establishing an online sharing platform called Beranda Inspirasi and Alun-Alun Inovasi where SDGs actors can share their experiences and best practices on SDGs related projects
4. Support the development of 2021 VNR Report 5. Support dissemination of 2021 VNR

1. Website and mobile apps of BASADA developed and adopted by Pekanbaru city government 2. Minister of National Development Planning presented 2021 Indonesian SDGs VNR during high-level political forum in New York, infographic and video produced 3. Beranda inspirasi and Alun-Alun Inovasi established
4. Provision of one (1) Expert consultant for SDG 16: Consultant had engaged and contributed substantively to the consultations and drafting of the VNR’s and analysis, particularly for Goal 16: dedicated to the promotion of peaceful and inclusive societies for sustainable development, the provision of access to justice for all, and building effective, accountable institutions at all levels. The report of Goal 16 contains the progress towards achieving the goals and objectives
5. Provision of one (1) Expert consultant for SDG 17, along with translation of two (2) Indonesia Voluntary National Review 2021 documents. This Consultant had helped drafting the VNR on the progress of Goal 17 (and interlinked with other relevant Goals, trade-off, and spill over) and on means of implementation.
6. Production of one (1) Indonesia Voluntary National Review 2021 Video.
7. Production of one (1) Indonesia Voluntary National Review 2021 E-Infographic.
N/A Note : Activities for this output concluded, therefore the remaining fund will be re-phased to output 00119800.
N/A

condition.narrative: UNDP as the Implementing Partner will comply with the policies, procedures and practices of the United Nations Security Management System (UNSMS.)

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

comment.narrative: Activity Result 3.1: SDGs Academy Indonesia curriculum developed. Activity Result 3.2: SDGs Academy Indonesia venue arranged and facilities made available. Activity Result 3.3: SDGs Academy Indonesia Learning Management System developed. Activity Result 3.4: All 3 programs Leadership certification, Mobile learning and Study abroad, and other SDG related talks/seminars/conferences implemented and conducted.

Preparation and advocacy made for the readiness to Launch the 1st model of SDGs Learning which engaging students, in 2021. The SDG learning will comprise a mobile learning and group-class session. Due to COVID 19, the plan for the launch and following implementation would be prioritized on learning platform using mobile /virtual sessions. Its notable to prioritise the mobile learning platform, in ensuring the engagement of youth as well as to consider theyre one of the risk group to be protected from dangerous exposure of coronavirus.

1. At least 2 regular batches of Leadership Certification Program (LCP) conducted - this LCP includes local government, NGOs, academics, private sector and other SDGs actors 2. 10 online learning modules on various topics, such as poverty reduction and sustainable waste management, developed and launched on MOOC of SDG Academy 3. At least 1 webinar and 3 SDGs knowledge sharing facilitated through SDG Academy social media platform

1. 60+ SDGs Certified Leaders graduated from 2 batches of LCP (1 batch in Mar to Jul 2021 and 1 batch in Aug to Dec 2021) - among the leaders were local development planning agencies, woman with disability and national philanthropy agency 2. 13 online learning modules developed and launched on MOOC of SDG Academy - among the topics are poverty reduction, sustainable waste management, sustainable investments, human resources and employment and SDGs 101 3. 2 national-level webinars and 4 SDGs knowledge sharing facilitated through SDG Academy social media platform - additionally, more than 3 writing competitions on SDGs topics conducted

1. At least one (1) new resource mobilisation realised with private sector 2. At least 1 regular batch of Leadership Certification Program (LCP) and 1 tailored LCP conducted - this LCP includes local government, NGOs, academics, private sector and other SDGs actors 3. At least 1 new online learning module added in the Mobile Learning Program 4. Payment gateway mechanism for paid-learning program identified 5. At least 1 webinar facilitated through SDG Academy platform

1. 43 participants graduated as “SDG-Leaders. For the 1st time the Graduation was conducted in Hybrid format on 1 August 2022 Topics: Sustainable Waste Management & Human Resources and Employability
2. *Data as per August 2022: 4,367 additional course enrollment since September 2021 for SDG Mobile Learning Program
3. Knowledge Sharing Platform: 304 submitted articles and 288 published articles (209 additional articles September 2021), articles covering issues on 17 SDGs goals and the compilation of articles from writing competition
4. Outreach and Promotional Activities: 13 speaking engagements, 11 Instagram Live Talks, Including 4 SDG Talks (online and hybrid)
5. Partnership and Collaboration: SDG Academy Indonesia now embedding its Mobile Learning Program SDG 101 materials to the Lembaga Administrasi Negara (LAN) platform – ASN Unggul. The embedment is currently being reviewed by Academy team, the adoption of the Mobile Learning Program Material (SDG 101) into Ministry of Education, Culture, Research and Technology / DIKTI’s Platform - Kampus Mengajar - SPADA. And The Academy will embed GUYUB’s learning materials about Preventing Violence and Extremism (PVE) on its Mobile Learning Program. The materials targeting youth in an engaging and effective manner in pursuit of promoting SDG 16. Co-fund with GUYUB: Digitization process.
6. Resource Mobilization; New Financing Agreement (FA) signed with Donor Nippon Closures Company, Ltd. (NCC) - Japanese Private Sector on July 26, 2022 Area of collaboration: Capacity building on Circular Economy (CE) Total contribution : ¥ 20,000,000 / US$ 145,317 Timeframe : 2 years
7. Payment Mechanism: DS can be used as a payment instrument for SDG Leadership Program service. The type of development services provided by the SDG Academy Indonesia is developing training methodology/curricula and delivering training courses for the SDGs. The Development Service can potentially be the solution for the Academy’s prospective Tailored SDG Leadership Program (collecting fees from a company/institution), in which UNDP shall provide the development services and deliverables to assist the recipient in accelerating their existing project(s). SDG Leadership Program is facing a challenge in payment mechanisms for individual enrolment participants. Although the team had explored the best mechanism on how to collect fees from individual participant(s).
8. Through the prototyping work, SIP in Gorontalo was able to map the social economic condition of the poor fishermen and their sources of livelihood. The project was able to advocate further improvement and capacity development of the targetted vulnerable group. The poor fishermen/women group was promoted and passed through the Ponelo Village Head decree. Such condition guarantees the fishermen/women existence and village government support toward their livelihood, which will be institutionalized in the village government programs. Furthermore, after receiving series of capacity strengthening activities, fishermen/women are more knowledgable in improving the quality of their catch, managing the post-harvet production, understanding the market price and aware of the conservation issues among sea-cucumber cultivation.

comment.narrative: 1. Informal cooperation under ATSEF, and conceptualization of ATS governance mechanism outlined in Ministerial Declaration 2. NIMCs loosely formed, with no clear mandate for ATS priority concerns 3. No SAP implementation finance secured by governments and development partners 4. Priority actions in the NAPs are not mainstreamed in national and local policy and programming frameworks 5. Limited local knowledge on integrated approaches 6. There are no coordinated regional climate change assessment efforts addressing regional coastal and marine concerns in the ATS region 7. No countries that are implementing specific measures from the SAP 8. No management plans and appropriate measures implemented for rebuilding or protecting fish stocks including alternative management approaches 9. Aru, Indonesia: Approx. 775 registered fishing vessels in Aru operating in the red snapper and shrimp fisheries (combined). 10. South Fly, PNG: 2 tons per year dried fish maw (bladder) produced. 11. Aru, Indonesia: Approx. 775 registered fishing vessels in Aru operating in the shrimp and red snapper fisheries; 775 vessels (<30 GT) which do not have VMS; 1400 vessels using gillnet gear. 12. Merauke, Indonesia: Approx. 500 registered fishing vessels operating in the barramundi fishery in Merauke. 13. South Coast, Timor-Leste: Approx. 150 registered vessels in the south coast municipalities. 14. South Fly, PNG: Approx. 2700 households involved in small-scale fishing 15. Oil and gas development is expanding in the ATS region, but local communities lack awareness and capacity to respond to marine pollution incidents 16. Indonesia: Southeast Aru MPA METT: 39 17. Timor-Leste: NKS NP METT: 24 18. No threatened species under enhanced protection 19. No coastal areas are currently under ICM; Timor-Leste is currently preparing ICM plans with support of PEMSEA 20. 1. Finalize recruitment process of regional project management personnel. 2. Organize and conduct RSC meeting in coordination with Timor-Leste as host. 3.Secure consultant to develop political and legal analysis of ATS region. 4.Conduct consultations for on political and legal analysis at NCUs or NPBs. 5.Secure consultant to develop TOR and guidelines for SPF. 6. Organize and conduct SPF back to back with RSC. 7. Consultant under no.3 to conduct regional institutional review in ATS and present initial result at RSC. 8. Consultant under no.3 to undertake assessment of political economy of regional in ATS region and present initial results to RSC. 9.Secure consultant on climate change to undertake regional review of climate change with national climate change professionals and present initial results to RSC. 10.Secure consultant on EAFM to collate baseline data and present initial EAFM plan at RSC. 11. Secure consultant on IUU fishing to update baseline estimates on IUU fishing in ATS and review national policies and legislation on IUU with initial result to be presented at RSC. 12. RPMU to participate in RPoA-IUU subregional committee meeting and coordination committee meeting. 13. Secure consultant on marine and land-based pollution to carry out assessment at regional and Indonesia pollution hotspots with initial result to be presented a RSC. 14. Secure consultant to undertake review and gap analysis resulting to a regional profile of ecosystem assets, connectivity and evaluation pressures, with initial assessment report to be presented at RSC. 15. Secure a consultant to design ATS MPA Network with initial draft to be presented or consulted at RSC. 16. Consultant under no.15 to develop regional plan of action for enhanced protection of marine turtles to be presented at RSC. 17. Coordinate cross-country visits if possible. 18. Secure consultant for project monitoring and evaluation and assist RPMU Policy and Resource-based Specialist in initial research and preparation of draft framework for ATS SAP Monitoring Mechanism. 19. Secure Communications Consultant to develop Stakeholder Engagement and Communications Plan for presentation at RSC. 20. Communicate project results and findings at RSC. 21. Develop and maintain contacts database. 22. Organize regional exchange activities if possible. 23. Develop, launch and regularly update ATSEA website. 24. Circulate e-newsletter via project social media sites. 25. Participate in various international and regional events and share ATSEA work and accomplishments.

In 2020, the project management has been completed on board, with special procedures and measures followed the COVID 19 protocols. The regional PMU start operating in Denpasar, Bali. The RSC meeting was conducted virtually in early Dec 2020, participated by project country members.

Component 1 Regional and National Governance for LME Management 1. Finalization of the regional governance model, including TOR and guidelines for the Stakeholder Partnership Forum (SPF). 2. Initiation of the ATS Transboundary Diagnostic Analysis (TDA) update. The report will be finalized in 2022. 3. Completion of the Climate Change (CC) Vulnerability Assessment Report. 4. Development of the Gender Equity and Social Inclusion (GESI) and assessment of Social and Environmental Safeguards (SES).
Component 2. Improving LME Carrying Capacity 1. Finalization of the regional thematic assessment reports related to climate change, IUU fishing, marine pollution, and habitat and biodiversity conservation. 2. Progress made on the development of EAFM plan. 3. Organization of the webinars and workshops in support of the different thematic studies.
Component 3 Knowledge Management 1. Launched an enhanced website and social media platforms supported by more regular postings. 2. Publications of the Project Annual Report, quarterly newsletters, press releases, and short videos on the project results and campaign. 3. Participation and dissemination of the project results in relevant virtual events.

Building on the experiences from 2020, particularly on the changes and challenges brought about by the global pandemic, the ATSEA-2 project has demonstrated better adaptive management in the year 2021 with majority of its regional target activities and outputs completed.
Under Component 1 on Regional and National Governance for LME Management, bulk of the efforts were accorded to the consultation process for the ATS regional governance mechanism. Recognizing the need to build country ownership and to facilitate better consensus-building, the original work plan for finalization of the regional governance model, including TOR and guidelines for the Stakeholder Partnership Forum (SPF), was recalibrated to allow for wider and deeper engagement of stakeholders in all four ATS countries. Thus, building on the regional governance assessment report, further national consultations have been completed with initial results to be presented at the 3rd RSC meeting in December 2021. Updating of the ATS Transboundary Diagnostic Analysis (TDA) has been initiated in November and will run up to November of 2022. As for climate change targets, the final Climate Change (CC) Vulnerability Assessment Report together with a Decision-making guidance toolkit has been completed, and shared through a Climate Change workshop. The toolkit has also been applied in the development of case study on impact of CC to fisheries in selected sites. Surveys and reports related to Gender Equity and Social Inclusion (GESI) and Social and Environmental Safeguards (SES) have also been completed.
Component 2 on Improving LME Carrying Capacity has also shown significant progress with the completion of all regional thematic assessment reports related to climate change, IUU fishing, marine pollution, and habitat and biodiversity conservation. While the development of EAFM plan would require further extension to secure more primary data, though alternative platforms such as online surveys, virtual FGDs of Advisory Forums, and deployment of enumerators have been carried out to address some of the limitations posed by COVID. The initial target of using LIPI’s research vessel was also cancelled due to COVID restrictions as well as organizational changes. LIPI and ATSEA-2, however, agreed to explore other technical collaboration in support of fisheries data gathering and management. Several webinars and workshops in support of the different thematic studies have also been conducted. As Component 2 requires several field activities that were affected by COVID restrictions, target regional exchange visits had to be postponed and moved to 2022 (i.e., Rights-based management fisheries visit to Australia).
In terms of Knowledge Management under Component 3, the project is on track on all targets except for the cross-project visits that were also affected by COVID restrictions. In 2021, the project has launched an enhanced website and social media platforms supported by more regular postings. Numbers of viewers and subscribers to ATSEA pages have also steadily increased. Several publications such as the Annual Report, quarterly newsletters, press releases, videos, etc. have been completed, posted and/or disseminated to a wider audience. ATSEA-2 has also actively participated and co-organized several virtual forums or events.

1. Recommendations and decisions on key ATSEA-2 initiatives, including Work Plan and Budget for 2022, reached at RSC Meeting 2022. 2. Design and action plan for the operationalization of Regional Governance Mechanism, including inputs from legal, administrative and financial assessment on operationalization of the mechanism approved. 3. SPF work plan 2022 implemented and reviewed during quarterly meetings. 4. Financial Landscape Assessment Report/Guidance Document published and disseminated. 5. GESI Action Plan implemented and results monitored and reported to RSC 2022 and as part of project M&E reporting. 6. Familiarization on Climate Change Adaptation (CCA) tools and use of CCA Decision-making Guidance Toolkit promoted through conduct of Regional trainings on CCA. 7. Updated ATS TDA Report. 8. Regional EAFM Plan endorsed by RSC 2022. 9. Updated IUUF baseline data compiled, shared and considered in TDA updating. 10. Updated Marine pollution assessment compiled and considered in TDA updating. 11. MPA Network Design endorsed by RSC and proposed roadmap considered in TDA and SAP updating. 12. ATS SAP Monitoring and Reporting System developed building on ToC, pilot tested, and adopted for future performance monitoring. 13. Project Finding and Outputs packaged through various media including ATSEA website and shared at key ATSEA meetings. 14. Engagement of key media groups in ATS region strengthened through fellowship program and selected events. 15. Updated Stakeholder Engagement and Communications Plan guiding the communication and outreach activities of ATSEA2.

1. The Ministry of Maritime Affairs and Fisheries (MMAF) is committed to developing 30 million hectares of marine protected areas by 2030. Indonesia already has 28.4 million hectares of marine protected areas spread over different areas. Kolepom Island in the Merauke district- of Papua Province is one of the pristine marine habitats and become threatened by unmanaged fisheries from a different area. UNDP with the ATSEA-2 project supports the province and district government to facilitate the first marine protected area (MPA) in Papua not just conserve marine biodiversity, provide livelihood and food security for the coastal community. In 2022, ATSEA-2 already facilitated serial capacity buildings for local government and universities to establish MPA and continue with the initiation of new MPA manage by the province with Governor of Papua Decree Number 188.4/295/2019 to conserve MPA in Kolepom island and allocate in province marine spatial planning (RZWP3K). UNDP actively supported the data collection for the zonation plan, and focus to have awareness of the important role of conservation areas in the communities. By the year 2022, ATSEA-2 Project with the zonation working group manages to arrange the serial public consultation and technical consultation to finalize the zonation plan document as part of the legal draft of MPA establishment. On 9 December 2022, the final legal draft has been approved by bureau law under MMAF to be signed by the Minister of Marine Affairs and fisheries to establish 356,337.9 Ha to conserve demersal fisheries (Snapper and croaker), shrimp, and sawfish habitat in the estuarine ecosystem that will contribute to 2.637.565 ton/years of fisheries resources in Arafura and provide direct livelihood and food security for 7251 community from 11 villages inside the Kolepom MPA island. 2. Positioning in area of ocean-based solutions for environmental protection. With the Climate Promises financial support and UNDPs coordination, UNDP provided technical support in the development of the Roadmap for Marine Sector Mitigation Actions. The roadmap is expected to guide efforts to increase contributions to reduce emissions in the sector. In addition, Guidance for Climate Change Vulnerability Assessment focusing on marine and coastal ecosystem in the Arafura and Timor seas was developed to understand climate vulnerability at a local scale and to help communities to prepare for and respond to climate-driven impacts and identify effective and targeted adaptation measures.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1.# health units reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 2.# educational establishments reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 3.# of service users that benefit from rehabilitated/ reconstructed health facilities (men and women; girls and boys) 4.# of school-age children that benefit from reconstructed or rehabilitated educational facilities 5.# tones of municipal solid waste sustainably disposed of and/or recycled per day, using rehabilitated facilities and newly introduced waste management systems 1.6 # of cubic meters of earthquake and tsunami debris recycled by municipal public facilities by the end of the project

comment.narrative: Heavily damaged buildings by the earthquake and government assessment damage and losses have deemed the building as unsafe and unsuitable for use. Hence, we have defined the buildings as zero although there was a building on the site before the earthquake.

1. 5 health units reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 2. 9 educational establishments reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards

1. Partially achieved - 14 health units approved as target for reconstruction and DEDs are completed. Construction works are expected to be completed in 2021. 2. Partially achieved – 25 educational establishments approved as targets for reconstruction and DEDs are completed. Construction works are expected to be completed in 2021.

1.10 health units reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 2. 18 educational establishments reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards

1. Partially achieved - 10 out of 14 subprojects have been completed by the second semester of 2021 and four remaining targets are still in progress. 2. Partially achieved – 4 out of 25 educational facilities have been completed by 2nd semester 2021 and the remaining 21 subprojects are still in progress.

1.10 health units reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 2. 25 educational establishments reconstructed or rehabilitated using ‘build-back better’ construction standards 3. 2 sanitary landfills reconstructed to enable sustainable disposal of municipal waste

1. Partially achieved - 13 out of 14 subprojects have been completed by the second semester of 2022 and one remaining target is still in progress and will be completed by 2023. 2. Partially achieved - 7 out of 25 educational facilities have been completed by 2nd semester 2022 and the remaining 18 subprojects are still in progress. 3. 2 sanitary landfills reconstructed to enable sustainable disposal of municipal waste in progress.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1.# km of irrigation canals and drainage in disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture (Donggala and Sigi) 2.# of culverts and bridges disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture (Donggala and Sigi) 3 # local markets rehabilitated or reconstructed 4 # households that benefit from economic infrastructure rehabilitation (men and women), including direct beneficiaries (self-employed; employees) and indirect beneficiaries (consumers/users) – in retail and agriculture 5# newly created jobs in the waste management and recycling ecosystem (collection, sorting, processing retailing), direct and indirect, men and women

comment.narrative: Heavily damaged buildings and economic infrastructure caused by the earthquake and government assessment damage and losses has deemed the building as unsafe and unsuitable for use. Hence, we have defined the buildings and beneficiaries as zero although there was a building and there were beneficiaries on the site before the earthquake.

1. 4 km of irrigation canals and drainage in disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 2. 2 of culverts and bridges disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 3. 1 local market rehabilitated or reconstructed

1. Partially achieved - 6 community infrastructures related to function of irrigation canals and drainage approved with estimated length of 3,1 km; rehabilitation/ reconstruction works is planned to be started in 2nd semester of 2020. 2. Partially achieved – Two bridges approved; DEDs are in progress. 3. Partially achieved - Genggelang village local market reconstruction is approved; DED is completed, and construction planned to start in early 2nd semester of 2020.

1. Cumulative 4 km of irrigation canals and drainage in disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 2. Cumulative 2 culverts and bridges disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 3. Cumulative 3 local market rehabilitated or reconstructed.

1. Partially achieved - 8 (Eight) community infrastructures related to function of irrigation canals and drainage approved with estimated length of 4 km 2. Achieved - 2 subprojects completed. The farmer bridge Belunak in East Lombok completed in December 2020 and Tuva suspension bridge in Sigi district completed on in April 2021. 3. Partially achieved - 1 of 3 subprojects completed. Genggelang village local market reconstruction has been completed and handed over to the community for immediate utilization while awaiting formal administrative asset transfer to GoI. Remaining rehabilitation of 2 local markets in Central Sulawesi are in ongoing reconstruction process and expected to be completed by early 2022.

1. Cumulative 4 km of irrigation canals and drainage in disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 2. Cumulative 2 culverts and bridges disaster-affected areas rehabilitated or reconstructed for improved agriculture. 3. Cumulative 3 local market rehabilitated or reconstructed.

1. Achieved - 8 (Eight) community infrastructures related to function of irrigation canals and drainage approved with estimated length of 4 km completed by first semester 2022. 2. Achieved - 2 subprojects completed. The farmer bridge Belunak in East Lombok was completed in December 2020 and Tuva suspension bridge in Sigi district was completed in April 2021. 3. Achieved - 3 subprojects completed. Genggelang village local market reconstruction has been completed and handed over to the community and formally transfer the asset to GoI. 2 local markets in Central Sulawesi have been reconstructed by the 1st semester 2022.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: PMU staffs recruited and established, advisory committee meeting per annum

comment.narrative: The PMU was established specifically for this project hence baseline is zero
PETRA PMU performed the project implementation amidst limitations due to COVID-19 pandemic.
PMU functioning with new approaches in managing implementation rate and monitoring progress delivered by project partners, mainly through online communication.
PETRA PMU performed the project implementation amidst limitations due to COVID-19 pandemic. Project implementation progress consulted with PETRA Project Board at least once in 2021

PMU functioning with new approaches in managing implementation rate and monitoring progress delivered by project partners, mainly through online communication. Project Board meeting conducted online on 30 June 2021.
PETRA PMU performed the project implementation amidst limitations due to COVID-19 pandemic. Project implementation progress consulted with PETRA Project Board at least once in 2022

As of 2022, 7 Districts in Central Sulawesi and NTB provinces are being supported for its recovery interventions 9 Provinces supported for COVID-19 recovery needs assessment, Contingency planning and recovery planning. 1 District in Bali Province is being supported to enhance its tsunami preparedness inputs through a Regional Project, Tsunami Preparedness Phase III, ongoing until Dec 2022.
54 public service and community infrastructures were designed in line with the build forward better and safer approach, with improved accessibility for women and people with disability. Women were also involved in the construction work. This achievement is expected could improve the minimum standard for public and community services in post-disaster areas to withstand future disasters. in 2022, 33 out of 54 total targeted facilities had been reconstructed and handed over to GoI for further maintenance and utilization which contribute to the recovery of education services to students in 7 schools, health services in 12 healthcare facilities, and helps community restore their livelihood through the reconstruction of 13 community infrastructures.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

Indicator 2 Number of initiatives to monitor the implementation and the financing of the National Action Plan Indicator 3 Number of collaborative actions with non-taskforce stakeholders for broader implementation Indicator 4 Number of policy, SOP, and regulations on waste management and marine debris handling developed Indicator 5 Number of activities to support the development of waste management infrastructure and its operationalization, circular economy implementation, as well as targeted awareness-raising and clean-up activities for targeted stakeholders Indicator 6 Number of policy briefs informing progress made and what stakeholders can accomplish Indicator 7 Number of likes on awareness campaign materials e.g., short video for YouTube, Instagram posts

1. Prepare annual report on the progress and achievement of the marine debris handling to the President of Indonesia 2. Initiatives (e.g. monitoring tool, proposal, etc) to monitor and finance the implementation of the National Action Plan for Marine Debris Handling 3. Nine collaborative actions with non-taskforce stakeholders 4. Two policies, SOPs, and regulations on waste management and marine debris handling 5. Nine activities to support the development of waste management infrastructure and its operationalization, circular economy implementation, awareness-raising and clean-up activities for targeted stakeholder 6. One policy briefs on waste handling, management, and utilization products 7. 750 likes on campaign contents on marine plastic debris awareness on social media platforms

1. Annual report on the progress and achievement of the marine debris handling to the President of Indonesia have been prepared 2. Initiatives to monitor and finance the implementation of the National Action Plan, such as: a. The operationalization of the reporting system. Through this system, the project was able to monitor the achievement of the ministries’ activities and report them to the Chairwoman of Implementing Team for National Action Plan for Marine Debris Handling. b. Three evaluation meetings for the ministries’ PIC for the reporting system have been held. Through this meeting, the representatives of the ministries updated each other on their respective ministry’s achievements and future plans. c. integrated national land-based and sea-based waste data platform to ease the calculation process of reduction achievement of the national action plan implementation have been developed. 3. 18 collaborations with relevant non-governmental stakeholders to conduct joint activities. The stakeholders are: Maritim Muda Manado; Ikatan Bujang Dayang Bangka Barat; Gerakan Indonesia Diet Kantong Plastik (GIDKP); Gojek; Merdi Sihombing, Ecofashion Designer; Mata Cinta; Minderoo Foundation; ASDP; World Bank; Asian Development Bank; KfW; OSEAN; KOEM; and Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries of South Korea. In addition, the secretariat in collaboration with the Ministry of Religion, the Indonesian Ulema Council, and the Mosque Council, has also launched the Interfaith Indonesian Waste Charity Movement (Gerakan Sedekah Sampah Indonesia, GRADASI). Until today, GRADASI has 6 pioneer mosques and 50 branch mosques that have gathered and sold 30 tons of household waste. 4. Coordination meeting with Pelindo III and ASDP have been carried out in April 2021, in which waste data from Pelindo III and ASDP will be integrated with the national platform. In addition, The Secretariat also convened coordination meeting with World Bank in June 2021 to enrich the baseline study in an effort to reduce marine debris leakage and in September 2021 which discussed marine debris handling policy. 5. Four books have been written, 20 Coordination meetings, 20 webinars, 15 Instagram Live, Ending Plastic Pollution Innovation Challenge (EPPIC) Phase II, and Ending Plastic Pollution Through Comic and Writing Contest for A Better Indonesia (EUPHORIA), Youth Forum on Waste Awareness (Forum Pemuda Peduli Sampah), and serial workshop of Interfaith Waste Charity Movement have been held to support the circular economy implementation, and awareness-raising. 6. A policy brief regarding the use of trash booms in the 10 most plastic-polluted rivers to prevent waste leakage into the ocean of Indonesia have been published. 7. Social media campaign on marine plastic litter awareness (Twitter/Instagram/Facebook), by creating campaign contents on marine plastic debris awareness on social media platforms: Instagram: @tkn_psl, facebook: TKN PSL, twitter: @tknpsl, where there are 28,320 likes in 2021.

1. Completion of annual reports on the progress and achievement of the marine debris handling to the President of Indonesia 2. Six Initiatives (e.g. monitoring tool, proposal, etc) to monitor and finance the implementation of the National Action Plan for Marine Debris Handling 3. Nine collaborative actions with non-taskforce stakeholders 4. Two policies, SOPs, and regulations on waste management and marine debris handling 5. Nine activities to support the development of waste management infrastructure and its operationalization, circular economy implementation, awareness-raising and clean-up activities for targeted stakeholder 6. Two policy briefs on waste handling, management, and utilization products 7. 1000 likes on campaign contents on marine plastic debris awareness on social media platforms

1. On 28 January 2022, the project conducted Mangrove Clean-up in Mangrove Conservation Forest area in Denpasar, Bali. The event was jointly conducted with Ministry of Environment and Forestry and was attended by the local government, 30 local communities/NGOs, and fishermen. In the event, the project also installed trash booms to prevent waste leakage into the sea. 2. Launch of the Church-Based Interfaith Indonesian Waste Charity Movement (Gerakan Kolekte Sampah) in collaboration with ADUPI, Mayora, and Danone. Covening webinars and clean-up action in collaboration with some stakeholders in commemoration of National Waste Awareness Day 2022 3. 5 coordination meetings have been held with taskforces in 2022 to enhance discussion and dialog to address challenges of marine plastic litter 4. To reach a wide audience, the project in collaboration with Ministry of Communication and Information held two live sessions about marine debris handling and waste management on one of Indonesia’s national television channels, Kompas TV. 5. Social media campaign on marine plastic litter awareness by creating campaign contents on marine plastic debris awareness on social media platforms: Instagram: @tkn_psl, facebook: TKN PSL, twitter: @tknpsl, where currently there are 595 posts, 5,335 Instagram followers, and 1,020 likes in 2022.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

comment.narrative:
Around 70% of remaining HCV forests under threat of further fragmentation, particularly in Sumatra due to oil palm cultivation (this figure will be validated for each site in Year 1)
Around 29.091 hectares Elephant Corridor in the Landscape Seblat. Meanwhile, of the six ecoregions in Sumatera, some are still in good condition based on the amount that is included in the classification of essential ecosystems. Sumatran tropical pine forest and Sumatran montane rain forest has the largest area of essential ecosystems, sized 76.87% and 63.76%. While the smallest ecoregion area in an essential ecosystem is the Sumatran freshwater swamp forest with only the size 8.80%.
Essential ecosystems in Sumatra reach around 12.8 million hectares or 30.37% of the total area of Sumatra. Only 26.18% of this area is represented in the conservation area, while the rest is divided into areas: protection forest (25.32%), production forest (19.11%), and limited production forest (16.47%).
Approval of the full-size project proposal by the GEF Council
The GEF has approved the project on 10 June 2021 for full implementation.

1. Completed project inception workshop in Q2 2022. 2. Established Project Management Unit in Q2 2022. 3. Created Project Special Accounts and Fund Flow Arrangements 4. National validated of the key actions and strategies for conservation of yellow-crested cockatoo. 5. Drafted strategy and action plan - conservation of the Sumatran elephant. 6. Assessed current state of forest patrolling system and law enforcement capacity in the respective project sites. 7. Developed key actions to simplify and standardize protected areas information management

The project has not yet delivered any result due to the delays on project commencement. The project has not yet achieved any concrete project output, but it is designed to strengthen management of multiple use landscapes to enhance biodiversity conservation, generate sustainable land-use and livelihood practices and address illegal wildlife trade. Achievement of the project objective will contribute in building capacities of national and sub-national institutions, and communities to sustainable manage of natural resources focusing on terrestrial ecosystems, habitats, and key endangered species conservation.

comment.narrative: No capacity at provincial level to support DNA-based investigation related to IWT
The capacity ta provincial level to support DNA-based investigation related to IWT established

The Veterinary Faculty at Syiah Kuala University in Aceh Province is expanding its DNA lab to enhance genetic studies. While the lab is not currently designed for forensic purposes related to illegal wildlife trade (IWT), the pathology lab at Syiah Kuala Veterinary School actively collaborates with the Ministry of Environment and Forestry's Regional Office (BKSDA) to assist in IWT investigations.

Indicator 14: Increase in income of community members that includes women headed households from community enterprise. -High-value products from NTFPs such as coffee, cacao, turmeric, durian, rattan fiber, candle nut, and rattan fruit (“Dragon’s Blood” dye)=. -Status of community development fund
This list will be refined during initial consultation with local communities Baseline incomes of households are as follows: Ulu Masen between USD 750-1980/year (Source Socioeconomic and Gender Profiles of North Sumatra 2016) Bengkulu around USD 750/year (Source: Senoaji 2009) Moyo island USD 865 – 1,500/year (Source: Achmand, B and Diniyati D 2018). The variance in earnings within landscapes is dependent on livelihood sources. These figures will be validated for the project sites during the community consultation process in Year 1

Target 2021: 1) 2,964 households 2) 50 people (30% female) 3) 9 institutions, operational 4) 2 national workshops.

1. UNDP in partnership with te Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources through the Accelerating Clean Energy Access to Reduce Inequality (ACCESS) project funded by KOICA, has supported the poor and most vulnerable communities to have equitable and sustainable access to essential services for improving their livelihodd in 23 villages in 4 (four) provinces in Indonesia (East Nusa Tenggara, West Sulawesi, South-East Sulawesi, and Central Kalimantan provinces) and 25 villages in three municipalities of Timor-Leste (Municipality of Dili (Atauro), Bobonaro and Manatuto) through technical support, evidence-based policy advice,knowledge management, capacity building and partnerships. In 2022, the notable achievements are as follows: a. Built capacity of 50 local operators from Indonesia (46% women) and 30 local operators from Timor-Leste (26% women) to learn the operations of renewable energy infrastructures. b. Procurement and distribution of 992 solar home systems to 25 villages, benefiting 4,541 people (44% women). c. Establishment of 21 Village Owned Enterprises (covering 23 ACCESS target locations) that will manage the built renewable energy infrastructures. d. 21 Village regulations on electricity tariff issued by local governments.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1,200 MSMEs supported for business revitalization

comment.narrative: n/a
Funding active 1 April 2021

- 1,000 MSMEs in Jakarta, Bandung and Surabaya supported for business revitalization. - 30% (of 1,200) MSMEs adopt circular economy in their business activities. - 400 Ultra-micro/micro enterprises capacitated on green economic recovery, digital economy and inclusion through technical assistance from CSOs.

In the last quarter of 2021, ASYX has been selected to develop modules, recruit and train 1200 MSMEs in 3 metropolitan areas of Jakarta, Bandung and Surabaya. By the end of 2021, 1,293 MSMEs from the four sectors of food and beverages, textile, furniture and handicrafts, have been selected as participants. Through FGDs and consultation with MoCSMEs and other relevant stakeholders, the modules are being finalized as of end of December 2021. Delay in the achievement of 2021 targets was mainly due to refining the deliverables and determining the approach by consultation with several relevant stakeholders. One of the biggest challenges was in the selection of participants/MSMEs and the actual realization of it during the implementation process. The training program of MSMEs is expected to be completed in first quarter of 2022, hence, the remaining targets of 2021 will be carried forward to 2022 and also refined on several particular aspects, as described in the 2022 target section below.

- 30% (of 1,200) MSMEs gained knowledge on circular economy practices relevant to their business activities. - 400 Ultra-micro/micro enterprises capacitated on green economic recovery, digital economy and inclusion through technical assistance from CSOs. - provision of OHS material to 1,293 MSMEs - provision of ICT equipment for 200 MSMEs - 3 prototypes of waste management in traditional market.

- 1 set of BISNIS LESTARI module - 1,462 MSMEs learnt BISNIS LESTARI in two rounds of training. Of which, 361 participants are able to formulate company profile with GUSTI indicators (Digitally agile, Profitable, Circular, Resilient and Inclusive). 25 best selected MSMEs to present their business plan to potential investors. 172 MSMEs attended a follow up training on Digital Literacy. - 1,293 packages of OHS were distributed

Reached 11,359 partcipants through capacity-building activities. This included 7,838 offline par6cipants (3,505 Party Stakeholder and 4,333 Non-Party Stakeholder) and 3,521 online par6cipants (850 Party Stakeholder and 2,671 Non-Party Stakeholder), with strong female engagement across all sessions

title.narrative: National institutions for transparency-related activities in line with national priorities are strengthened

description.narrative: National institutions for transparency-related activities in line with national priorities are strengthened

comment.narrative: 0 region
National institutions for transparency- related activities in line with national priorities are strengthened in all 5 targeted regions

National institutions for transparency-related activities were strengthened through technical training on sub-national GHG inventory, capacity building on climate risk coding, monitoring of PROKLIM sites, coordination for BTR1, integration of systems such as SRN and SPECTRUM, coordination on carbon trading, and multi-stakeholder engagement, while equitable sub-national outreach was ensured through five regional hubs in Sumatra, JABALNUSRA (Java, Bali, Nusa Tenggara), Kalimantan, Sulawesi, and Papua Maluku (Sumapapua), which together enhanced institutional readiness, sub-national ownership, and data flow into the na6onal transparency framework inline with Article 13 of the Paris Agreement

title.narrative: Relevant tools, training, and assistance for meeting the provisions stipulated in Article 13 of the Agreement are provided

description.narrative: Relevant tools, training, and assistance for meeting the provisions stipulated in Article 13 of the Agreement are provided

comment.narrative: 0 region
Relevant tools, training, and assistance for meeting the provisions stipulated in Article 13 of the Agreement are provided in all 5 targeted regions

Relevant tools, training, and assistance were cumulatively provided across five key regions (Sumatra, JABALNUSRA, Kalimantan, Sulawesi, and Maluku-Papua) to support the implementa6on of Article 13 of the Paris Agreement. This included technical training on GHG inventory, MRV systems, and IPCC guidelines; assistance in sub-national risk coding and institutional reporting; and deployment of tools such as the SRN and SPECTRUM platforms. Through Training of Trainers (ToT), FGDs, webinars, and technical support, both Party and Non-Party stakeholders were equipped to contribute effectively to the Enhanced Transparency Framework at national and sub-national levels

title.narrative: Improvement of transparency over time is assisted

description.narrative: Improvement of transparency over time is assisted

comment.narrative: Quality of MRV System: 2 (scale 1-10) Measurement systems are in place, but data is of poor quality and/or methodologies are not very robust; reporting is done only on request or to limited audience or partially; verification is not there.
Quality of MRV System: 8 (scale 1-10)

Improvement of transparency over 7me was assisted through progressive capacity-building efforts and technical interven6ons at na6onal and sub-na6onal levels. CBIT Indonesia supported the enhancement of the MRV system through training on GHG inventory using the 2006 IPCC Guidelines, QA/QC procedures, and periodic repor6ng. Technical assistance was provided for MRV development in forestry, energy, and waste sectors, and support was extended for the integra6on of sub-na6onal repor6ng into na6onal plarorms like SRN and SPECTRUM. Addi6onal measures such as spot checks, financial audits, and data compila6on for BTR-1 further reinforced the transparency framework and strengthened long-term verifica6on mechanisms

title.narrative: Institutional Capacity for Climate Transparency Strengthened

description.narrative: Institutional Capacity for Climate Transparency Strengthened

comment.narrative: Qualitative assessment of institutional capacity for transparency: 1 (scale 1-4)
Qualitative assessment of institutional capacity for transparency: 4 (scale 1-4)

Institutional capacity for climate transparency was strengthened through the establishment of a dedicated organizational unit with permanent staff responsible for coordinating transparency-related activities. The capacity-building program included targeted trainings, workshops, Focus Group Discussions (FGDs), and Training of Trainers (ToT) sessions for stakeholders and academics in land-based, non-landbased, and climate adaptation sectors. Technical improvements featured the development and pilo6ng of Quality Assurance/Quality Control (QA/QC) documents and local emission factors to enhance emissions repor6ng accuracy. Addi6onally, the use of SIGN-SMART and SRN PPI systems facilitated expanded GHG inventory repor6ng across five regions. These initiatives have been integrated into na6onal planning and budgeting frameworks, ensuring sustainable and institutionalized climate transparency efforts

title.narrative: Capacity Building and Long term strategy for Government and NPS (non Party Stakeholders) Climate Transparency are Strengthened

description.narrative: Capacity Building and Long term strategy for Government and NPS (non Party Stakeholders) Climate Transparency are Strengthened

comment.narrative: Qualitative assessment of institutional capacity for transparency: 0 (scale 1-4)
Qualitative assessment of institutional capacity for transparency: 4 (scale 1-4)

Advanced capacity building and long-term strategy development for the Government and Non-Party Stakeholders to enhance climate transparency. Key efforts included a Focus Group Discussion (FGD) on collaboration and funding strategies for sustaining the Climate Transparency Hub and Center of Excellence (COE). The inclusive participation of national, sub-national, academic, civil society, and private actors strengthened institutional capacity and supported the implementation of strategies for accessing finance and technology

title.narrative: Enhanced Quality Assurance (QA)/ Quality Control (QC) and verification processes as well as adoption of improvement plans.

description.narrative: Enhanced Quality Assurance (QA)/ Quality Control (QC) and verification processes as well as adoption of improvement plans.

comment.narrative: Quality of MRV System: 0 (scale 1-10)
Quality of MRV System: 8 (scale 1-10) Robust systems for GHG inventory and MRV Emission in compliance with Paris Agreement developed and established in 5 targeted regions.

A robust MRV system was developed and implemented across five targeted regions, addressing the initial lack of structured QA/QC and verification processes. This system now includes formalized QA/QC procedures and adopted improvement plans, significantly enhancing data accuracy, consistency, and credibility. These efforts directly align with the Paris Agreement and have strengthened GHG emissions transparency at both national and sub-national levels

title.narrative: Domestic MRV System completed, including capacity building for data survey and data collection and public awareness in implementing methodology to support MRV

description.narrative: Domestic MRV System completed, including capacity building for data survey and data collection and public awareness in implementing methodology to support MRV

comment.narrative: Quality of MRV System: 0 (scale 1-10)
Quality of MRV System: 0 (scale 1-10) Quality of MRV System: 9 (scale 1-10) By Q14, National Registry Sytem for Adaptation Scheme is completed

CBIT Indonesia completed key steps toward developing a domes6c MRV system by conduc6ng capacity-building ac6vi6es like training, technical guidance, workshops, FGDs, and coordina6on mee6ngs to enhance public awareness and technical skills for data survey and collec6on. Collabora6on with government directorates included mul6ple review mee6ngs and technical consulta6ons to develop necessary guidelines and tools. Focused trainings and discussions on MRV for local waste sector mi6ga6on under the Carbon Economic Value framework further strengthened local government capacity

title.narrative: NDC Implementation and Tracking Progress Strengthened

description.narrative: NDC Implementation and Tracking Progress Strengthened

comment.narrative: 0 regions
NDC Implementation and Tracking Progress Strengthened in 5 targeted regions

Continued to enhance the implementation and progress tracking of Indonesia s Nationally Determined Contributions across five targeted regions. Key activities included technical training, Training of Trainers (ToT), Focus Group Discussions (FGDs), and technical assistance aimed at strengthening the capacity of regional stakeholders and Non-Party Stakeholders (NPS), including provincial governments, NGOs, and the private sector. A major achievement was the update of the NPS contribution mapping, which provided a comprehensive overview of stakeholder roles in achieving NDC targets. These activities also reinforced coordination and consistent reporting mechanisms between national and sub national levels, and promoted collaborative approaches to monitoring and evaluating climate actions, in line with the global transparency framework

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

comment.narrative: 1. Baseline on direct project beneficiaries to be established in year 1. 2. Baseline on area of restored land will be determined in year 3 (after ILM plans endorsed). 3. Baseline for targeted landscape management to be improved will be established in year 2 (after HCV/HCS assessment completed and potential area for improved management & set-asides identified). 4. Baseline on GHG to be determined in year 2 (after HCV/HCS assessment completed and potential area for improved management & set-asides identified)

1. Initiated sub-national systems leadership programme based on capacity needs assessment. 2. Engaged two off-takers to demonstrate initial public-private-community collaboration and partnerships to strengthen sustainable production and sustainable value chains for selected agricultural commodities. 3. Conducted ESIA and SESA, and development of the ESMP management plan. 4. Conducted project inception workshop and reporting. 5. Conducted project board and steering committee meetings as well as annual stakeholders workshop 6. Operational of the PMU including hired key technical experts.

There is no concrete project/output level result has been achieved yet during this PAR Reporting period. Project document was signed in 21 February 2022, and the Inception Workshop was conducted by end of August 2022, followed by series of coordination and landscape verification process in five districts under five provinces through multistakeholder and inclusive process. The project has been involved in several online activities with the Global FOLUR team. The objectives are to: 1) increase knowledge and exchange learning on the topic related to green commodities and gender aspect of the Food Agriculture and Commodity System; 2) Building coordination and networking with the global FOLUR team. Through these activities, the project FOLUR Indonesia team has been trained on several aspects related to managing the project related to green commodities, including building trust with stakeholders and adaptive learning. The network has also been established with the global FOLUR Team, which can provide technical support and advice as relevant for successful FOLUR project implementation, including gender approach to project implementation which has been tailored to FACS approach.

description.narrative: 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level. 1.2 Diversity of different data sources used 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented 1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP) 2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners 2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite 2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies 3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network. 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network. 3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.

comment.narrative: 1.1 - 2 new sources of data per year 1.2 - 1 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 6 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 2 innovation methods per Action Plan 2.1 - 1 blogs, or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 - At least one government entity or partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods. 2.4 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 13 interaction by the end of year - 3.2 - 5% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Network´s work is featured at least 1 time per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. - The Lab team engages at least once a week across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. - Identify and engage with 2 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

Acclab Indonesia was only fully operational in November 2020 when all three Heads were onboard. The AccLab team was participating in dedicated 4-week AccLab Bootcamp in October 2020. The Acclab team was introduced to the CO team in Programme Staff Meeting and also conducted internal collective intelligence session with CO, both on December. The CO has identified the frontier challenge of the Lab which is on urbanization and smart city, as well as identified key government partner, which is Ministry of Research and Technology. The team also expanded its initial network through active engagement with unusual partners such as SDG Office under Regional Government in Jakarta and Design Ethnography Lab in Bandung Institute of Technology.

1.1.- 5 new data sources per year 1.2 - 6 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 35 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 5 innovation methods per Action Plan. 2.1 - At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 - At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method. 2.4 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network.48 interaction per year 3.2 - 25% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Networks work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. - The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. - Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

Throughout 2021, the Accelerator Lab dedicated its efforts to strengthen the role of UNDP as a leader in Indonesia’s innovation ecosystem through broadening its network with various government stakeholders (mainly The National Planning Ministry – BAPPENAS and The National Research and Innovation Agency - BRIN), academic institutions (Design Ethnography Lab in Bandung Institute of Technology), civil society organizations (RUJAK Centre for Urban Studies and seven grassroot communities in different cities), private sector (Ecoxyztem Venture Builder) and other UN entities (UNESCO and UNDRR Office for Asia and Pacific). The team conducted lean research with BAPPENAS to influence policy design processes on flood mitigation, comprising of a public survey and ethnographic study, resulting in a community-based policy brief. In addition, the team also initiated and co-organized a capacity building initiative entitled ‘Futures Thinking in Disaster and Resilience’ with UNESCO and UNDRR which consisted of webinars, participatory workshops, and public dialogues, inviting youth and young professionals throughout Asia Pacific. Lastly, the team also conducted the Urban Innovation Challenge, together with Ecoxyztem and supported by BRIN, that crowdsourced grassroots solutions on waste management (with the support of RESTORE project), MSME digitalization and smart city – which will lead to a 6-months experimentation phase with the selected winners. In regards to programme integration, the team supported various initiative by other CO project such as DX4R for the InaRISK Hackathon Festival, DGPRU – PVE on behavioural insights, and conducted a CO-wide initiative on ‘Deep Demonstration’ with the support of Strategic Innovation Unit and BRH, among others.

1.1.- 5 new data sources per year 1.2 - 9 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 50 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 10 innovation methods per Action Plan. 2.1 - At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 - At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method. 2.4 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year 3.2 - 50% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Networks work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. - The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. - Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

CO has been championing innovation to anticipate future shocks and testing new ways of delivering impact. Through forward-looking approaches such as strategic foresight, CO supported the National Ministry of Development Planning to identify progress gaps in SDG achievement due to COVID-19 and signals of change that potentially impacts the key priority areas in the country. In addition, CO has been supporting the government on inclusive policy making in urban resilience through ethnographic approaches and mapping local solutions, gaining bottom-up insights from impacted communities. The focus on innovation combines the regulatory environment and SDGs maximization into the investment plans, ensuring it is embedded in the implementation. Beyond new approaches, CO also introduces market-based instruments and strengthens the innovation ecosystem through innovative partnerships, from collaborating with national and subnational governments as well as with ‘unusual actors’ such as venture builders, universities, think tanks, MSMEs and grassroot communities across the country.
With the focus on urban resilience, the Lab conducted a digital mapping initiative to collect real-time data on lakes as part of the resilience infrastructure against flooding, which utilized open-source platforms to increase government data quality and better information access for the public. Through the Urban Innovation Challenge (UIC), the Lab implemented pilot projects on smart city that resulted in a web-based portal for disaster preparedness, supported MSME to build an integrated online platform while delivering social impact and developed a waste management ecosystem on food loss and waste in traditional markets. Integrating these insights with the CO’s multi-sectoral expertise, the Lab established research on the New Capital City with the National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN) to utilize strategic foresight to gain bottom-up insights from local communities to complement existing plans and regulations from the national government. a.b. For SDG Achievement, with the SDG Integration team, the Lab designed and facilitated the SDG Push Dialogue with the Ministry of National Development Planning (BAPPENAS) as part of the broader Acceleration Diagnostic process, incorporating forward-looking approaches to identify gaps in current interventions and opportunities for program and policy for Indonesia 2045. Key achievements include the ability to bring new sources of data - incorporating spatial data and qualitative survey - that was possible due to the specific expertise of partner on data collection and openness of government to leverage novel digital platforms, conduct lean experiments with grassroots across multiple sectors that was feasible through close coordination with the Lab and influence government partner to use forward-looking approaches for strategy that derived from similar visions on anticipating future shocks

comment.narrative: Indonesia successfully received funding from the Green Climate Fund (GCF) under its REDD+ Results-Based Payment (RBP) pilot program for 20.25 million tco2eq of emission reductions achieved from deforestation under its REDD+ program for the period 2014 to 2016 through the implementation of the National REDD+ Strategy (STRANAS).
The preparation of Prodoc, LoA with partners and other documents to set up the project
There is a first draft of Prodoc but still needs revision. LoA and FAA are signed. Performance-based Payment Agreement (PBPA) is under discussion with government partners
Prodoc and project initiated. PMU is ready. Readiness and capacity building under Output 1 are accelerating the achievement of Output 2.
The Prodoc has been signed and project has been initiated and implemented. PMU is ready. Readiness and capacity building under Output 1 are accelerating the achievement of Output 2.
The implementation of REDD+ enabling activities in Indonesia continues accelerating the achievement of Output 2. the PBPA signed and the first disbursement of Output 2 transferred.

1.Through REDD+ RBP Project, UNDP support the GoI in compiling and finalizing 2 important ministerial regulations: 1) Ministerial Regulation of the Ministry of Environment and Forestry No. 21/2022 on the Procedures for Implementing the Carbon Economic Value and the Ministerial Regulation No. 10/2022 on the Preparation of a General Plan for Forest and Land Rehabilitation of Watersheds and Annual Plan for Forest and Land Rehabilitation. The first one is important as it will be the basis of Rumah Karbon, carbon trading, and NDC. It will also regulate the type of emission reduction activities outside NDC that can be traded. The second regulation is no less important as all of the forest and land rehabilitation activities will be based in the MoEF and UNDP assisted the preparation of the regulation. 2. UNDP support the GoI in improving the National Strategy Document (Stranas). The latest document in 2022 will set the direction for the sustainable forest management programmes 2021 - 2030. Currently the document is being finalized and will be uploaded in the first quarter 2023. 3. REDD+ RBP project also improved and assisted the development of the National Registration System (SRN). SRN has been applied as the integrated system of the overall mitigation activities (on emission reduction) and adaptation activities (increased resilience). The project integrated the achievement of emission reduction and SIS (safeguard information system) of REDD+. 4. UNDP has been facilitated the payment of the non-reimbursable fund from GCF to the Indonesian Environment Fund (IEF) by using UNDPs Performance Based Payment (PBP) modality. In December, GoI will receive USD 47 Million for its stewardship in climate action and conservation following the success in its forest protection and conservation in the 2014 - 2016. The 47 million USD marks the first disbursement of the Indonesias Result-based payment for REDD+ project which will be a total of USD 103 million from the GCF. Parts of the funds will go towards the implementation of Indonesias Social Forestry Programme which aims to alleviate poverty, halt deforestation, and end forestland conflict by empowering local communities to manage forests themselves. All of the activities will contribute to the countrys FOLU Net Sink 2030 which is a pivotal component of the countrys enhanced NDC.
