1. To increase the use of waqf in dealing with environmental issues with real socio-environmental impacts, UNDP, Indonesian Waqf Board and the Green Waqf Movement Team have developed the Green Waqf Framework. The Green Waqf Framework serves as a global reference on Islamic finance from Indonesia, which has demonstrated actions on advancing Islamic finance for sustainable development and addressing the climate crisis. It covers comprehensive information on Waqf and climate change initiatives, involving the government, private sector and grass-root communities. 2. UNDP provided technical support to the Ministry of Finance (MOF) in the development of the Insurance and Risk Finance Facility (IRFF). This document was launched in October and it lays the necessary groundwork for Indonesia to address multiple crisis such as changing climate, natural disasters, and the pandemic stalling development and exacerbating inequalities. 3. The Blue Financing Strategic Document was launched by the Coordinating Minister of Maritime and Investment Affairs and UNDP on 12 October. The document aims to unravel the economic potential of marine-based economic activities and explore the capital market possibilities for investment in the blue economy. The document will become a reference for the government to issue blue bonds/sukuk in the coming years. 4. The United Nations team in Indonesia, through a joint UN program – Accelerating Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) Investment in Indonesia (ASSIST) – led by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), kicked off the implementation of the Blue Finance Accelerator program, together with the Asian Development Bank (ADB) and the Indonesian Coordinating Ministry of Maritime and Investment Affairs.(4). Under the Blue Finance Accelerator program, UNDP developed and implement the venture accelerator program to scale up participating startups and SMEs through capacity-building support, increased access to finance, and involvement in blue sector projects. The Blue Finance Accelerator program aims to enhance the Government of Indonesia’s commitments and capacities for the Blue Economy and to leverage innovative financing instruments toward infrastructure programs supporting ocean preservation, community resilience, and the overall growth of the Blue Economy. 5. Role of private sector is immense – SDG Investor Map was successfully launched, and private equity Impact Fund has been developed in cooperation with the Indonesia Chamber of Commerce and a private fund management operator. 6. The UNDP supported the GOI to strengthen public finance management (PFM) through the development of a carbon tax roadmap study and ESG framework of PPP projects. Concretely, the UNDP helped the GOI to leverage alternative financing sources for the SDGs, whereby over USD 6.8 billion was raised from the Green Sukuk issuances since 2018 (including the USD 1.5 billion from the recent issuance in 2022), and the Indonesia Impact Fund (IIF) deployed USD1 million equity investment to 2 SDG-oriented startups. Furthermore, UNDP supported the GOIs SDG Bond issuance where EUR 500 million was raised in 2021 and USD 210 million in 2022, which proceeds are mobilized towards social sectors including education and health. The assistance extends to the development of impact report for both issuances. These initiatives are contributing to closing the USD 4.7 trillion financing gap.
Youth Co:Lab Bootcamp 2022 Youth Co:Lab programs have always strived to foster gender equality through encouraging more and more young female entrepreneurs to participate in all the training proceedings. This year, in collaboration with PLUS, almost 50% of the participating enterprises are led by young women (12 enterprises to men’s 13). We’ve also encouraged our participants to foster women empowerment and social inclusion in their business process, to ensure that they leave no one behind.
Youth Co:Lab Indonesia National Dialogue 2022 The National Dialogue is an important part of Youth Co:Lab’s presence in Indonesia, as this is where we can engage policy makers and government representatives to advocate for a host of issues, ultimately on youth development but also taking into account issues of gender equality and social inclusion. Multiple panel sessions discussed how youths can play a bigger role in tackling these issues, and recommendations offered to ministerial partners highlighted the same issues to ensure that youth voices on them are heard and included. UNDP IFL Youth Team played a crucial role to connect Ministry of Youth and Sport, Ministry of National Planning, Coordinating Ministry for Human Development and Cultural Affairs, British Council, and UNFPA to stand together coordinating on how youth development roadmap should implement in Indonesia through policy and programming.

Output 1.1 A research using behavioural insight approach conducted to identify means for preventing and responding to GBV in line with the human rights principles
1. The final version of the research proposal has been developed and approved by the Behavioral Insights team. The Behavioural Insights research proposal paves the foundation in research implementation that focuses on exploring the barriers to help-seeking faced by survivors of gender-based violence (GBV) in DKI Jakarta Province, Indonesia. The study seeks to: 1) understand help seeking behaviour of GBV survivors in areas with high rates of GBV cases; 2) describe the patterns of help-seeking; and establish the challenges and barriers faced by survivors of GBV in seeking help.
Output: 2.1. Data collection and analysis of BI approach and findings are ongoing and expected to be completed in January 2023
The recruitment of a consultant to provide advisory and the recruitment of 3 senior field researchers have been completed in October 2022. UNDP conducted a two-day briefing programme in November 2022 and invited the Research Team, together with the BIT. The 2 day briefing consists of Technical Briefing and Psychological Approach Briefing. Data collection is confirmed to start during late November to early December 2023. In addition, contract amendment with BIT was initiated in November 2022 with BRH to include analytical service to the findings from the research. Awareness raising on BI approach and GBV service provision at Urban 20 side event
UNDP recognizes the Urban 20 (U20) as a powerful knowledge sharing platform for city leaders to collaborate in addressing socio-economic and environmental barriers to foster sustainable and inclu- sive economic and social recoveries post COVID 19. Against this background, the U20 played a pivotal and triggering role for gearing up further political attention and commitment to increase protection for women, children and other vulnerable groups from the threats of Gender Based Violence by the international community at large. U20 and its members could promote more policy coherence at international level, more effective cooperation and stronger technical capability, inter alia by advo- cating evidence-based recommendations, information and valuable elements on GBV prevention and eradication towards its own constituency, as well as to larger audiences. Recognizing the critical needs to address GBV in promoting inclusive social recovery for all, UNDP and the Government of DKI Jakarta jointly facilitated knowledge sharing and outreach at U20 2022 side event on 30 August 2022 at Fairmont Hotel. The side event also served as information sharing on the the use of BI approach as innovative initiave to promote behavior change, while also maximising outreach to strategic stakeholders on integrated service center as part of UNDP GBV prevention and eradication in the country.

During this reporting period, the project is still in the inception phase. Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MoFA) as the main Government counterpart of this project continuously postponed conducting the 1st PSC Meeting as MOFA is very much occupied with the preparation for G-20 event throughout this year. The postponement of the PSC Meeting led to the delay of UNDP’s project implementation especially related to UNDP’s assessment at sub-national level through Low-Value Grant Agreement (LVGA). The TOR for the CSO has already been finalized since August 2022, however, according to UNDPs POPP, we could not proceed with call for proposal for CSO, before conducting the Project Board Meeting (or PSC). In this case, the selection process of the CSO, such as the selection criteria, must be approved, and agreed by the PSC members during the 1st PSC. It also affects other UNDP’s activities, because the other activities will be shaped as per the findings of the initial assessment, which will be carried out through the CSO.
Considering this situation, PMU is maintaining regular coordination with MOFA to monitor the discussions within the ministry and encourage immediate follow-up to facilitate the implementation of activities, particularly on the pending PSC. The PMU also initiated direct coordination with relevant line ministry, such as Ministry of Home Affairs, to ensure timely implementation of project activities.
PMU tried to find the alternative ways to proceed the call for proposal without having to wait for the PSC meeting. After having several discussions with UNDP QARE Unit, there is an alternative to proceed the call for proposal without having to wait for the PSC meeting i.e., by revising the project document. Accordingly yhe PMU incorporated the selection process of the CSO along with the selection criteria to the revised project document. The revised project document is now in the process of approval from the donor, the fund Management Unit of the Migration Multi-Partner Trust Fund (MMPTF FMU).

description.narrative: The Joint Programme will conduct and produce an assessment to identify the policy gap on migration at the sub-national level with analysis on how the local governments of Indonesia have been dealing with the influx of migrants due to COVID- 19, including considerations of gender sensitivities across the policies.

description.narrative: To explore financing possibilities for migration, a dedicated feasibility study on innovative finance mechanism with gender sensitivity on migration in Indonesia will be conducted, and further consulted with stakeholders on how to materialize it based on the feasibility study.

title.narrative: Facilitating whole of society dialogue around the National Action Plan, with inclusion of CSOs/NGOs

description.narrative: There are many stakeholders in migration issues, not only government counterparts but also CSOs, research institutions and academia. By having regular roundtable discussion on the topic related to NAP on migration with many actors, the Joint Programme will also contribute to the coordination and information/experience sharing with national actors, including government, CSOs, academia and development partners. This will also strengthen the whole of society approach in the process of establishing network on migration in the country.

title.narrative: Provide support to develop local policies which is aligned with the GCM and develop technical guidance to integrate migration into local governments’ strategic plans and policies, including guidance on GCM budgeting

description.narrative: Following the workshops and trainings, the Joint Programme will provide support to local governments to develop their local policies which is aligned with the GCM. The assessment to identify the policy gap on migration at sub-national level with analysis on how local governments have been dealing with influx of migrants due to COVID-19, which will be conducted at the beginning of the project, will serve the basis of intervention. Referring to the Guidelines on mainstreaming migration into local development planning developed by UN JMDI, Technical Guidance will be developed with stakeholders at local government level to facilitate discussion to integrate migration into their strategic plans and policies. This guidance will also include the guidance on GCM budgeting.

title.narrative: National Symposia on Migration Best Practices

description.narrative: In addition, the Joint Programme will support the government in organizing National Symposia on Migration Best Practices. The Symposia will host multi-sectoral government and non-government stakeholders from different localities in Indonesia to share their successful practices, lessons learned, and innovation on migration policies and programming, particularly those that are related to the protection and empowerment of Indonesian migrant workers as the main priority of Indonesia in the GCM.

title.narrative: Facilitating inter-agency cooperation and coordination, including bilateral meetings on the alignment of SDGs and RPJMN to the development and implementation of the GCM National Action Plan, ensuring an effective, gender-responsive and human right-based approach

description.narrative: To support the development and implementation of the GCM National Action Plan, the Joint Programme will facilitate the inter-agency cooperation and coordination, especially to discuss how the National Action Plan could be made aligned with the SDGs and the National Mid-term Development Planning (RPJMN). The activity will include review on how SDGs and RPJMN programming in Indonesia already or not incorporating the migration lens.

1. Considering that the project just started on 15 Augustus 2022 and Inception Workshop on 5 and 6 October 2022, it is too soon to register the results of activities in the fields. 2. To ensure the progress and performance, the project developed Operational Community-Grant Guidelines and Grievance Mechanism, and Standard Operational Finance for SGP-7 Implementation. 3. The project launched a Grievance Mechanism aiming at providing easily accessible procedure for grantee partners to address any issues or concerns they may have regarding the implementation of activities and the management of grant funds. 4. Conducted Call for Proposal for Developing Landscape Strategies for the four-target landscapes. The Secretariat published a request for proposals on October 7-25, 2022 inviting national consortiums of NGOs/ Educational institutions/ Research institutions to participate in the preparation and development of Strategic Landscape Documents in four locations. PMU received three proposals at the time. However, only one proposal was considered for the National Steering Committee member evaluation process. The proposal passed to the next stage because it met the criteria, namely that the activities were carried out by more than one institution and they would work in four geographic locations. 5. Draft of Memorandum of Agreement (MoA) between YBUL (as the Implementing Partner) and the selected institution to conduct Landscape Strategies was prepared for the signatures of both parties. The selected institution is expected to start developing the Strategic Landscape Document immediately after signing the MoA in the end of 2022. 6. Prepared Baseline Assessment Report: the Secretariat (PMU) prepared baseline assessment reports for the project landscapes in Central Java and South Sulawesi, including updated information on priority areas for biodiversity conservation, rehabilitation of degraded land, priorities for renewable and clean energy among local communities, opportunities for introducing or enhancing alternative livelihoods for local people, and incorporating gender-responsive processes. 7. Participated in one SGP-UCP global workshop: PMU attended the online SGP-UCP global workshop on November 9, 2022. The keynote speaker was Sri Lanka’s National Coordinator. She shared her experiences as well as the learning process of GEF SGP-6 management in that country. 8. Selected and organized the National Steering Committee SGP-7 Members’ First and Second Meetings. The NSC member selection process started from July to September 2022. As many as 11 NSC members are determined from the process with a composition of government representatives, UNDP, YBUL, private sector, research/educational institutions, NGOs, other donors, journalists, etc. NSC first meeting was held on 22 September 2022 and the second meeting was on 11 November 2022 (for proposal screening). 9. Organized Project Inception Workshop: PMU organized two days the project inception workshop on 5 and 6 October 2022. In this workshop PMU together with UNDP CO, NSC members, and other stakeholders reviewed the project institutional arrangement, multi-year work plan, project results framework, gender analysis and gender action plan, stakeholder engagement plan, social and environmental screening procedure, global environmental contribution, etc. 10. Prepared, submitted and uploaded Project Inception Workshop Report into UNDP-GEF PIMS database on 16 November 2022.

2 GHG emissions cap- setting guidelines for coal power subsector of the energy sector and cement subsector of the industry sector

the Climate Promise project continue to support carbon market implementation by providing technical assistance in the preparation of GHG emissions capsetting guidelines on coalfired powerplants. This guideline is indispensable by the Government of Indonesian, especially the Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources (MoEMR) because of the obligation to provide GHG emissions cap-setting for power plants if the sector will implement the carbon trading. This is in accordance with the mandate of regulations regarding Carbon Pricing in Indonesia and successfully facilitated to develop the Roadmap 2024 2060 for Emission Cap and Allowance and Guidelines for Carbon Pricing Mechanisms Implementation to hire consultant company

The project focuses on livelihood and community development, encompassing social, economy, and disaster preparedness intervention. description.narrative:
Output 1 -- # targeted beneficiaries representing community groups (50% women) joined the introductory meeting with project team and village government.
Output 2 -- # men and # women farmers joined a set of training (agriculture, micro hydro, tourism, and creative economy).

comment.narrative:
Output 1 -- 0 targeted beneficiaries representing community groups (50% women) joined the introductory meeting with project team and village government.
Output 2 -- 10 men and 15 women participated at the previous workshop in the previous local economic development program.
Output 3 -- 0 monitoring conducted periodically. Output 3 -- 0

Output 1 -- 100 targeted beneficiaries representing community groups (50% women).
Output 2 -- 25 men and 25 women joined the training in each training activity ((agriculture, micro hydro, tourism, and creative economy.
Output 3 -- 3 monitoring activities conducted in coordination with counterpart and followed by reporting.

Livelihood & Community Development
UNDP and BAZNAS are collaborating in the Livelihood and Community Development (LCD) program by optimizing the local resources in Lubuk Bangkar Village as an effort to strengthen the post-Covid-19 community economy and the synergy of existing programs. It requires a cross-actor collaborative approach such as the local community, village government, and district government through related agencies and pays attention to gender mainstreaming by incorporating specific number of women in each activities. In doing so, socialization and FGD with local actors were successfully conducted as preliminary approaches. It was followed by a set of thematic activities (agriculture and women-youth empowerment). All the activities are conducted within the period of 27-28 September, 28, 29, 31 October, 1 November 2022.
Livelihood & Community Development
Working with the local CSO, UNDP-BAZNAS has carried out a programmatic approach to support the existence and sustainability of the potential in the village of Lubuk Bangkar optimally through enhanced economic sustainability and a strengthened policy environment. It started with building community and local government collective awareness followed up with conducting gender-disaggregated data assessment from farmers and plantation mapping, and identification of village’s potential, including but not limited to natural resources, human resources, culture, capacity, and women’s role to become integrated tourism, as well as organizing focus group discussion on the synergy of agricultural micro-enterprises groups with farmers. Particularly for the youth, the program provides organizational management and financial management training on local activities (i.e., printing and souvenir) as part of income generation efforts.
The principle of gender equality and social inclusion has been adopted through data aggregation on the beneficiaries prior to the implementation as well as in the planned activities such as FGD on the synergy of agricultural micro-enterprises groups with farmers. The involvement of women and youth during the pre-implementation (socialization) is part of the efforts to capture women and youth perspective and interest in related to the planned activities. From the local government side, there are also representative from women and youth from respective local offices. Women and youth are also mentioned as target explicitly in each activity.

title.narrative: HCFC Phase-out Management Plan for Indonesia – Stage III (Indonesia HPMP-III)

description.narrative: Indonesia is party to the Vienna Convention for Ozone Layer Protection and the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, hence it has agreed to meet the 2030 compliance targets by phasing-out the Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs), in accordance to the accelerated reduction schedule set by the Meeting of the Parties’ Decision XIX/6. Therefore, the Multilateral Fund for the Implementation of the Montreal Protocol (MLF) has approved, during the 92nd Meeting of its Executive Committee (ExCom), the Stage III of the HCFC phase-out management plan (HPMP) for Indonesia at a total cost of US $16,048,056, consisting of US $13,240,847, plus agency support costs of US $926,859 for UNDP; and US $1,685,000, plus agency support costs of US $195,350 for the Government of Australia (cooperating agency). The HPMP Stage III project will assist Indonesia to phase-out 184.59 ODP tonnes of HCFCs and allow the country to meet the Montreal Protocol compliance target of 100 per cent reduction by 2030 using a portfolio approach that builds from experiences and lessons learned from Stage I (2012-2018) and Stage II (2016-2024). The Stage III will expand training and certification schemes, continue raising awareness and improve knowledge base of sensitive sectors (Fisheries and Agricultural Cold Chain), promote low-GWP alternatives, improve circularity of refrigerants through recovery and reclaim processes, and estimate future servicing needs in light of the future servicing tail allowance (2030-2040). The key success factor for the Stage III lines on capacity building of servicing sector technicians as a critical means to improve capacities, reduce leakages and elevate the quality of services in general in the Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Sector.Upon the implementation of HPMP Stage-III, Indonesia will cumulatively reduced up yo to 5.96 million tonnes of CO2-eq.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
3,500 trained and certified technicians and reduced 184.59 ODP tonnes of HCFCs in accordance with allowed consumptions in Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) systems.

description.narrative:
The HPMP Stage III project is assisting Indonesia to train and certify 3,500 refrigeration and AC (RAC) technicians, and reduce and phase-out 184.59 ODP tonnes of HCFCs and allow the country to meet the Montreal Protocol compliance target of 100% reduction by 2030 except for those allowed for a servicing tail between 2030 and 2040, where required, consistent with the provisions of the Montreal Protocol, using a portfolio approach that builds from experience and lessons-learned from Stage I (2012-2018) and Stage II (2016-2024).
The project will have environmental impact through significant reduction in ozone-depleting substances (ODS) and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, contributing to the recovery of the ozone layer and mitigation of climate change.
The project will enhance the capacity of government agencies, private sector, and communities to manage and monitor the phase-out of HCFCs through training and technical assistance.
The project will encourage the Technology Transfer through the adoption of alternative, environmentally friendly technologies in the refrigeration, air-conditioning, and foam sectors.
The project will ensure strengthened policies and regulations to support the phase-out of HCFCs and ensure compliance with international environmental standards.

title.narrative: Enabling Blue Innovation Solutions for ASEAN’s Blue Economic Growth

description.narrative: This project will support the implementation of Blue Strategies #2 and #3, focusing on blue science, technology, innovation, and priority sectors. title.narrative: MTC ATM Project - Management and Technical Cooperation for the AIDS, Tuberculosis, Malaria Prevention and Control

description.narrative: The objective of this MTC ATM Project is to strengthened national and subnational level capacities to promote inclusive local development and service delivery for AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria. To achieve this, the project focus on two outputs, namely: 1. Effective Global Fund (GF) resources utilization for the intended purposes of project implementation, reporting, and closure that is more improved compliant with GF guidelines, policies, and procedures2. Improved national health system governance, procurement, and supply chain using empirical research and innovative solutions to ensure quality, timeliness, efficiency, and compliance

title.narrative: PPG Phase of Law Enforcement for Sustainable Viable Ecosystems and Biodiversity Resilience through Multi-sectors Engagement (LEVERAGE) - PIMS 9613

description.narrative: I. BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF THE INITIATION PLAN/GEF PPGObjective & Final OutputsThe objective of the GEF PPG is to develop the project concept into a full project entitled ‘Law Enforcement for Sustainable Viable Ecosystems and Biodiversity Resilience Through Multi Sectors Engagement (LEVERAGE)’. This is a child (national) project of the GEF Wildlife Conservation for Development Integrated Programme (WCD-IP). As described in the child project concept note, this project aims to strengthen collaborative and integrated law enforcement with multi-level stakeholder engagement for protecting biodiversity loss by incorporating positive behavioral change toward wildlife conservation and human health both men and women. The project is designed to strengthen law enforcement on wildlife crime to leverage benefits for biodiversity conservation and human health. The collective efforts of actors will reduce Illegal and High-Risk Wildlife Trade as a key driver of species loss and risk of pandemics. Simultaneously, it will address Human-Wildlife Coexistence issues through a multi-stakeholder and collaborative action approach. Application of technology and an innovative approach will be fostered. With GEF investment, the full-size project will improve management effectiveness of more than 600,000 ha of terrestrial protected areas, avoiding emissions from Agriculture, Forestry, and Other Land Use (indirect) - AFOLU sector approx. 23 million metric tons of CO2e over 20 years, benefiting 4,000 people including 1,200 women. Four project components and indicative outcomes and outputs are proposed to achieve the project objective:Component 1. Enabling Law Enforcement Ecosystems Outcome 1. Enhanced policy, regulatory framework and the governance for integrated wildlife-related law enforcement.Planned Outputs:1.1 Three policies recommendation on governance, policy and regulatory frameworks to strengthen law enforcement ecosystems.1.2. Developed transformative roadmap for effective co

A Gender Responsive Just Energy Transition Pilot Project

description.narrative: Capitalizing on the ACCESS project’s experience and investment, this project initiation aimed to prototype energy-economy activities by harnessing clean energy to empower women-led businesses, supported by digital literacy and financial inclusion, to reduce the gender gap in labor force participation and demonstrate the impact of the gender-responsive energy transition. The projects results will provide evidence for developing a policy brief and dialogue to influence the development of a gender-responsive just energy transition policy and program.

title.narrative: Building Resilience Development Governance (BRIDGE)

description.narrative: Through this Project Initiation Plan, UNDP aims to ensure efforts on adding value of results from its post-disaster rehabilitation and reconstruction interventions, contributing to enhancing local resilience which is key in Disaster Risk Reduction. While undertaking monitoring the Defect Liability Period of reconstructed health and education facilities in 19 locations in Palu Municipality, Sigi District, and Parigi Moutong District, UNDP continues its advocacy to respective local government commitments for maintenance and improvement of the facilities in public service delivery. UNDP foresees the need to provide technical assistance to improve risk awareness and increase local government integration and involvement in planning preparedness, response, and recovery to either natural or man-made disaster occurrences.

title.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – Phase II (BIOFIN INDONESIA)

description.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – BIOFIN – is a global programme that developed, piloted, and is continuously improving a methodology to measure existing biodiversity expenditure levels, assess future financial needs, and design comprehensive plans to use finance tools and solutions that increase financing, effectiveness and efficiency of biodiversity management in up to 132 countries. BIOFIN’s second phase enables up to 132 countries to complete the design and implementation of priority finance solutions and allowed a further 11 countries to undertake the assessments and create the finance plan. At the central level, knowledge management platforms will be expanded and additional guidance produced for most valuable finance solutions.

title.narrative: ELEVATE ; Enhanced Resilience via Integrated Approach and Digital Technology

description.narrative: An integrated Governance Approach is crucial to enhance public service delivery and optimize resource use across sectors. this approach brings together different levels of government, ministries, local authorities, and civil society to coordinate policies and strategies that are responsive to the diverse needs of Indonesias population. The Water-Energy-Food (WEF) Nexus offers a model for integrated resource management that acknowledges the interconnections between these crucial sectors. By fostering collaboration and breaking down silos in decision-making, this framework can help address disparities between regions and social groups, providing a foundation for inclusive development. It is evident that achieving integrated governance, enhanced service delivery and participatory planning are considered achievable by leveraging innovation and digital technology. By combining the WEF framework and digital innovation, Indonesia can improve public service delivery, enhance resources management and ensure that development benefits all citizens.

title.narrative: Activity web page
Institutional Strengthening for the Phase-out of ODS and HFCs in Indonesia

description.narrative: The Institutional Strengthening (IS) project is required to assist the Government of Indonesia to strengthen the capacity of relevant institutions and facilitate the work of sub national and local institutions on their roles to control and monitor substances that deplete the Ozone Layer and contribute to the Climate Change. The objective of IS project is to continue the support on integrating projects in the country and enhancing its achievements by supporting and ensuring the implementation of the Montreal Protocol programme in Indonesia. It will continue to: 1. deliver effective management, monitoring and enforcement on ODS phase-out activities including sustainability of ODS phase-out;2. continue the implementation and enforcement of the Ozone Depleting Substances legal framework; 3. provide coordination and oversight to complete the Stage II of the HPMP and advance the implementation of the Stage III; 4. continue the awareness outreach activities for active involvement of all stakeholders in sustaining ODS phase-out, HPMP implementation, KIP preparation and implementation. The project is expected to ensure (1) adoption/ implementation of ODS legislation and regulation to control and monitor ODS consumption. (2) efficient and timely data collection and reporting. (3) consultations and coordination with other national agencies/stakeholders. (4) supervision of timely implementation of phase-out activities and reduction in ODS consumption. (5) awareness raising and information exchange. (6) regional cooperation and participation to Montreal Protocol meetings. (7) integrateion of MLF gender policy in the implementation.

title.narrative: ASEAN Blue Carbon and Financing Profiling

description.narrative: The “Amplifying Blue Economy Growth through Enhanced Blue Carbon and Financing Profiling” project addresses the critical challenge of mitigating climate change and fostering sustainable development through the conservation and utilization of blue carbon ecosystems in ASEAN countries and Timor-Leste. These ecosystems, including mangroves, seagrasses, and peatlands, are powerful carbon sinks but are rapidly degrading due to urbanization, pollution, and climate change. Despite their potential to sequester carbon 35 times faster than tropical rainforests and support biodiversity, fisheries, and coastal resilience, their full potential remains underutilised.

title.narrative: PIMS 9613 LEVERAGE

description.narrative: Indonesia’s status as a biodiversity hotspot has been exploited by illegal wildlife trafficking, making this project a critical opportunity to bolster its role as a global leader in wildlife conservation. As part of the Global Wildlife Program (GWP) under GEF-8, the project builds on earlier GEF-financed and government investments to strengthen combatting the illegal wildlife trade through more effective law enforcement agency collaboration and enhanced engagement with local communities. By integrating conservation of globally significant biodiversity with human health and climate resilience, the project delivers multiple, synergistic benefits that extend beyond national borders. The initiative employs cutting-edge technologies, such as forensic tools and digital monitoring, alongside multi-stakeholder governance to reduce illegal wildlife trade — a key driver of species loss and zoonotic disease spillover. With GEF investment, this initiative will enhance the management of over 600,000 hectares of protected areas, avoiding 2.3 million metric tons of CO2 emissions over 20 years. It will also directly benefit 4,000 people, including 1,200 women, by facilitating behavior change through improved livelihoods and other incentives, strengthening conservation governance, and reducing human-wildlife conflict. In parallel, the project will incorporate targeted demand-reduction interventions, leveraging local cultural values and community engagement to address drivers of IWT, complementing enforcement and livelihood strategies. The five target protected areas in Sumatra harbor extensive globally significant biodiversity, including the Sumatran tiger, orangutan, elephant, rhinoceros, as well as pangolin, sun bear, and several species of songbirds.

title.narrative: PIP - ACCTRANS

description.narrative: The ACCTRANS project aims to accelerate Indonesia’s transition toward an inclusive and integrated low-carbon economy by catalyzing climate investment in the land use, land-use change, and forestry (LULUCF) sectors through gender-responsive planning and accessible finance, strengthening Sharia-based climate financing mechanisms, and establishing an integrated industrial emissions reporting platform for hard-to-abate sectors. The project will be implemented in selected regions with significant LULUCF activities and carbon-intensive industries, including Sumatra, Kalimantan, Sulawesi, and Java, focusing on improving both institutional and community capacity. To achieve its objectives, ACCTRANS will strengthen regulatory frameworks, promote inclusive stakeholder engagement, and leverage a mix of conventional and Sharia-compliant financing, while advancing robust data systems to support transparent emissions reporting and evidence-based policymaking. By integrating innovative finance, participatory approaches, and reliable data systems, the project ensures that climate action in Indonesia is effective, equitable, and aligned with the country’s long-term sustainability goals.

title.narrative: IDN-TCCF Plastics Circularity

description.narrative: The TCCF Plastics Circularity Project aims to address the critical issue of plastic waste management in ten countries across Asia. This initiative targets countries with varying levels of waste management infrastructure and plastic waste challenges. The project is designed to support the transition to a circular economy by improving plastic waste management, promoting recycling, and reducing plastic leakage into the environment. By focusing on these targeted areas, the project will contribute to better public health, environmental protection, and the development of sustainable waste management practices. The project is expected to contribute to collect at least 69,110 tons of plastics and support 135,600 direct beneficiaries during the implementation period across all ten participating countries. There are four components under the Project, namely: 1. Stakeholder Engagement and Strategy Development. 2. Implementation of Best Environmental Practices (BEP) and Best Available Technologies (BAT). 3. Strengthening National Capacities and Guidelines. 4. Information Outreach and Knowledge Management. The project will be managed through a two-tier structure comprising a Regional Component and National Child Projects in each of the nine participating countries.

title.narrative: Strengthening Police Capacities to Prevent and Respond to Technology-facilitated Gender-based Violence

description.narrative: Aligned with the flagship portfolio of UNDP Governance, Peacebuilding, Crisis, and Resilience (GPCR) global initiative which emphasizes strengthening the rule of law, promoting inclusive governance, and building resilient institutions to address emerging risks and vulnerabilities, the “Strengthening Police Capacities to Prevent and Respond to Technology-facilitated Gender-based Violence” project aims to enhance the institutional capacity of the Indonesian National Police and relevant stakeholders to prevent, investigate, and respond to technology-facilitated gender-based violence (TF-GBV). The project adopts a survivor-centered, rights-based, and coordinated approach, while also promoting whole-of-society approach that engages civil society, communities, and other key actors.The project was built on the previous joint effort with UNDP Seoul Policy Center (USPC) and Korean National Police Agency (KNPA) in January 2024 to March 2025 on “Police Capacity Building Support Programme for Combatting TF-GBV”, which laid the groundwork for institutionalizing a survivor-centered approach within law enforcement through the co-creation of TF-GBV training module in collaboration with the Indonesian National Police Education and Training Institute (Lemdiklat POLRI).

title.narrative: Boosting Renewable Innovation for Green and Inclusive Transition (BRIGHT)

description.narrative: Indonesia has committed to achieving Net Zero Emissions by 2060 or earlier, with interim targets of 23% renewableenergy by 2025 and 31% by 2050. Yet progress remains slow: renewables make up less than 15% of the energy mix,and solar power—despite its vast potential—contributes only marginally. Closing this gap requires a dual approach:unlocking large-scale financing for the transition while ensuring renewable energy reaches communities that remain underserved.

title.narrative: PIP - CREDDIN

description.narrative: The project aims to accelerate progress toward the 2030 FOLU Net Sink target by enhancing REDD+ implementation, improving governance for high-integrity carbon markets, and strengthening the government institution including Indonesian Environment Fund (IEF) as a channel for transparent, performance-based climatefinance. The project also supports the NDC implementation across priority sectors and selected provinces, with an inclusive approach that ensures meaningful and gender-responsive engagement of Customary Communities, local communities, and other stakeholders.

title.narrative: PIMS-10325 PPG GEF BIO-KARST

description.narrative: PPG supports the development of the project concept into a full project, Biodiversity and Inclusive Governance for Resilient Karst Ecosystems and Community Livelihoods (BIO-KARST), which aims to conserve globally significant biodiversity and deliver social and environmental benefits from Indonesia’s karst ecosystems. As outlined in the Project Identification Form (PIF), the PPG will support preparation of a technically robust, policy-aligned, and implementation-ready UNDP–GEF Project Document by strengthening the analytical foundation, refining the theory of change, and ensuring compliance with GEF and UNDP requirements. This includes refining project scope, outcomes, and implementation arrangements; validating priority intervention areas; integrating gender, social inclusion, and environmental safeguards; facilitating inclusive stakeholder consultations; strengthening institutional and financing frameworks; and ensuring alignment with national development priorities, biodiversity strategies, and the Kunming–Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. These activities will ensure the final project design is evidence-based, feasible, and positioned to deliver measurable global environmental benefits while supporting sustainable livelihoods and long-term governance of Indonesia’s karst ecosystems.
